proposals.title: Planning-Aware Incremental Processing with Latent Future Context for Document-Level NLP
proposals.motivation: Standard incremental processing in NLP, such as in summarization or QA, processes text sequentially with access only to past context. This creates a local coherence bottleneck, limiting a model's ability to anticipate key information or structure a global narrative. Overcoming this constraint is crucial for generating more coherent, informative, and faithful long-form outputs, which remains a core challenge in document-level understanding and generation.
proposals.hypothesis: By incorporating a lightweight, dynamically updated representation of anticipated future content and a global document plan into the incremental reading process, we can significantly improve the coherence, relevance, and factual consistency of generated text in tasks like summarization and long-form question answering.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture building on pre-2026 encoder-decoder transformers. A 'Planner' module, using a latent variable model, reads the document in chunks to produce a soft, evolving outline (a sequence of latent plan vectors). An 'Incremental Generator' processes text token-by-token, attending to both past context and the current plan vector from the Planner. The plan is updated iteratively as new document chunks are read, using techniques from hierarchical merging and uncertainty-aware retrieval to refine predictions. Training will involve a multi-task objective combining standard generation loss with a plan-prediction auxiliary task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for relaxing the strict incremental processing constraint, enabling more globally coherent text generation. It will yield new evaluation metrics for measuring long-range coherence and planning fidelity in generated text. The method will be empirically validated on long-document summarization and multi-hop QA, demonstrating improvements over strictly left-to-right and two-pass extract-then-generate baselines.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on two-stage summarization or static planning, our method integrates a continuously updated, latent future context directly into the incremental generation loop. This bridges the gap between rigid global planning and fully local incremental processing, a synthesis not explored in existing pre-2026 architectures.

proposals.title: Induced Token Relations: Formalizing Implicit Structures in Adaptive Memory Architectures
proposals.motivation: Modern language models increasingly use adaptive memory mechanisms, such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and context-aware merging, to handle long contexts and complex reasoning. While these methods improve performance, the implicit relations they induce between tokens—beyond standard attention—remain uncharacterized. Understanding these induced structures is crucial for improving model interpretability, verifying information flow, and diagnosing reasoning failures in tasks like question answering and summarization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adaptive memory and attention mechanisms induce a latent, weakly-supervised graph of token relations that is richer than co-occurrence or syntactic dependency, and that formalizing this graph can improve the controllability and verification of model outputs.
proposals.method: We will analyze models from Dr. Lapata's recent work, including context-aware hierarchical merging and decomposed summarization modules. Using probing classifiers and gradient-based attribution (e.g., Integrated Gradients), we will extract pairwise token relation scores. We will then formalize these relations using a graph neural network to learn relation types (e.g., 'supports', 'contradicts', 'elaborates') and validate them against human annotations on verified aspect-aware summaries and QA datasets with uncertainty quantification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for describing induced token relations, offering a new lens for model analysis. It will yield diagnostic tools for verifying information consistency in long-context tasks and could lead to more interpretable and controllable merging and summarization algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes attention weights or model internals, this proposal focuses on the *implicit relational structures* induced by adaptive, task-specific memory mechanisms—a novel abstraction for understanding how models integrate and reason with retrieved or merged context.

proposals.title: EdgeQA: Uncertainty-Aware, Modular Question Answering for Resource-Constrained Devices
proposals.motivation: Deploying language models on edge devices (e.g., phones, IoT sensors) enables real-time, private reading assistance but faces severe constraints on memory, compute, and power. Pre-2026, model compression often sacrifices reliability, and standard retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is too heavy. There is a critical need for systems that are not only small but also provide trustworthy, context-aware answers in low-resource settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A question-answering system for edge devices, built via decomposed, verified modules and equipped with lightweight uncertainty quantification, can achieve performance comparable to larger models while operating within strict resource budgets.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage architecture. First, we adapt the decomposed, aspect-aware verification from Lapata's summarization work to create a modular QA pipeline: a tiny retriever fetches relevant text chunks, a verifier module checks aspect relevance, and an ultra-efficient, heavily pruned transformer (e.g., a distilled MobileBERT variant) generates answers. Second, we integrate a lightweight, post-hoc uncertainty quantification method (inspired by Lapata's UQ work) using prediction confidence and retrieval score distributions to flag low-reliability outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end framework for trustworthy QA on edge devices, complete with open-source code and a benchmark for evaluating efficiency-accuracy trade-offs in mobile NLP. It will advance the field by demonstrating how modular verification and uncertainty estimation can be co-designed with model compression.
proposals.novelty: While model miniaturization is well-studied, the integration of decomposed, verified modules and explicit uncertainty quantification into a single, edge-deployable QA pipeline is novel. This moves beyond simple distillation by embedding reliability mechanisms directly into the compressed architecture.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Memory Networks for Coherent Reasoning Over Book-Length Documents
proposals.motivation: Current transformer-based models struggle with the context length required for book-length documents, leading to fragmented reasoning and loss of long-range narrative coherence. This limitation impedes progress in critical NLP applications like detailed literary analysis, legal document review, and longitudinal scientific synthesis. A scalable memory architecture is needed to effectively process and reason over hundreds of thousands of tokens while maintaining performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, modular memory system that compresses and selectively retrieves information at multiple granularities can enable coherent question answering and summarization over book-length contexts, outperforming existing long-context models on tasks requiring deep narrative understanding.
proposals.method: We propose a Hierarchical Memory Network (HMN) with three core modules: 1) A *Segment Encoder* using a pre-trained Longformer or BigBird to process individual chapters. 2) A *Context-Aware Hierarchical Merging* module (inspired by Lapata's prior work) that builds multi-level summaries (e.g., paragraph, scene, chapter) via learned compression. 3) A *Decomposed Retrieval* mechanism that, given a query, dynamically selects relevant memory levels and verified factual units (extending verified aspect-aware modules) to augment a smaller, fixed-window reasoning model. Uncertainty quantification will be applied to retrieval confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable architecture for book-length NLP, a new benchmark for evaluating long-document reasoning coherence, and a set of techniques for verified, hierarchical information compression and retrieval that mitigates hallucination.
proposals.novelty: The integration of hierarchical summarization as a dynamic, query-aware memory structure, combined with verified aspect-aware retrieval for factual grounding, moves beyond simple context window extension or naive retrieval-augmented generation for very long documents.

proposals.title: Dual-Objective Generation: Inverting Machine Reading Models to Produce Optimally Comprehensible Text
proposals.motivation: Current text generation models prioritize human-centric fluency, but the proliferation of AI-driven information processing (e.g., retrieval-augmented QA, automated summarization) necessitates text that is also optimized for machine comprehension. This is critical for improving the reliability and efficiency of downstream NLP systems, especially in high-stakes domains where model misinterpretation has consequences. The problem of generating text for a specific model's understanding remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting a pre-trained, frozen machine reading comprehension (MRC) model through a differentiable surrogate objective, we can generate text that maximizes the model's confidence in extracting a target answer or summary, thereby creating text that is 'easy to read' for that specific system.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-objective framework. First, we employ a large language model (LLM) as a generator. Its output is fed to a frozen, high-performance MRC or QA model (e.g., based on T5 or BART). We then define a novel loss combining: (1) a standard fluency/coherence loss from the generator, and (2) a *comprehensibility loss* derived from the reader model's output distributions (e.g., negative log-likelihood of a target answer span or summary token sequence). We will adapt techniques from uncertainty quantification in QA to weight the comprehensibility loss, ensuring the generator does not exploit model weaknesses. Training will use backpropagation through the differentiable surrogate of the reader's scoring function.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new task and evaluation benchmark for machine-oriented text generation. It will provide a general framework for inverting black-box reader models to improve human-to-AI communication. The resulting methods could enhance data synthesis for training, improve the robustness of human-written prompts for AI systems, and offer insights into model-specific textual biases.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in controllable generation targets style or sentiment, and adversarial attacks seek to fool models, this work uniquely inverts a reader model for *cooperative* generation, explicitly optimizing text for a specific AI's comprehension without sacrificing human readability. It merges concepts from model inversion and controllable generation in a novel, constructive objective.

proposals.title: Incremental Multimodal Reading: A Unified Simulator for Joint Text, Image, and Audio Processing
proposals.motivation: Current reading simulators and language models process information incrementally but are largely confined to textual input. Real-world comprehension, however, is inherently multimodal, integrating text with visual cues and auditory information. A simulator that can jointly and incrementally process these modalities is crucial for advancing models of human-like understanding and for building robust AI systems capable of handling complex, real-world documents and interactive scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a context-aware, hierarchical merging framework, extended to multimodal inputs, can effectively simulate incremental reading by fusing textual, visual, and auditory signals into a coherent and dynamically updated representation, leading to superior performance on multimodal question answering and summarization tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing incremental text reading simulator with a modular, decomposed architecture inspired by Verified Aspect-Aware Modules. Separate encoders (e.g., CLIP for images, Wav2Vec2 for audio) will process each modality. A hierarchical attention mechanism, guided by principles from Context-Aware Hierarchical Merging, will incrementally fuse these streams into a unified memory representation. Uncertainty quantification techniques from prior QA work will be applied to assess confidence in cross-modal inferences at each step.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first incremental reading simulator capable of joint multimodal processing, providing a testbed for studying cross-modal integration dynamics. It will advance methods for long-context, multimodal understanding and offer new evaluation paradigms for tasks like multimodal QA and summarization where temporal integration is key.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the incremental, hierarchical simulation paradigm—previously applied to text—to the asynchronous, heterogeneous streams of multimodal data, and in explicitly modeling the uncertainty that arises from fusing these distinct signals during incremental processing.

proposals.title: FastRead: A Cascaded, Uncertainty-Aware Model for Approximate Reading Comprehension
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) achieve high accuracy on reading tasks but are computationally expensive, limiting their use in latency-sensitive or high-throughput applications like real-time QA systems or preliminary document triage. Pre-2026, there is a clear gap in models that explicitly trade a controlled, predictable amount of accuracy for significant gains in inference speed, particularly for long-context tasks. This work addresses the need for efficient, 'good enough' reading models that can operate under strict computational budgets.
proposals.hypothesis: A cascaded model architecture that first uses a fast, approximate reader to handle straightforward cases and defers only high-uncertainty examples to a full, accurate LLM can drastically reduce average inference cost while maintaining acceptable overall task performance, as measured by a tailored speed-accuracy frontier.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage cascaded system. The first stage is a 'FastRead' module, a distilled, encoder-only transformer (e.g., a pruned DeBERTa) trained on a subset of reasoning chains for speed. It will be equipped with an internal uncertainty quantification module, inspired by Lapata's prior work on UQ for QA, to estimate its confidence. For inputs where confidence is above a tunable threshold, the FastRead model's answer is final. Otherwise, the input is passed to a second-stage, full-capacity LLM (e.g., GPT-3.5/4 or LLaMA). We will train and evaluate this system on datasets like QASPER and QuALITY, optimizing the cascade policy for the speed-accuracy trade-off.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel framework for building and evaluating approximate reading models, 2) a publicly released 'FastRead' model and cascade system benchmarked against standard baselines, and 3) a formal analysis of the speed-accuracy trade-off landscape for reading comprehension, defining new evaluation metrics beyond top-1 accuracy.
proposals.novelty: While model cascades exist, this approach is novel in its tight integration of a *purpose-built* approximate reader (not just a smaller LM) with task-specific uncertainty estimation for reading comprehension, and its explicit framing around creating a tunable, fast 'first-pass' reading model for document-intensive NLP pipelines.

proposals.title: Fused Summarization via Extractive Selection and Abstractive Refinement for Coherent Long Documents
proposals.motivation: Extractive summarization methods guarantee factual faithfulness but often produce incoherent, disjointed summaries. Purely abstractive methods improve fluency but risk hallucination and factual drift, especially on long, complex documents. A hybrid approach that strategically fuses extraction with minimal, targeted generation is needed to achieve both factual integrity and readability.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage model that first performs context-aware extractive selection and then applies a constrained, light-touch abstractive module to refine the selected spans will produce more fluent and coherent summaries than purely extractive baselines, while maintaining higher factual consistency than fully abstractive models.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture. First, a context-aware hierarchical encoder (inspired by Lapata's prior work) processes the document to score candidate spans. The top-k spans are selected, forming a factual backbone. Second, a lightweight, verified decoder module (extending ideas from decomposed opinion summarization) performs limited operations: rephrasing connectors, resolving coreferences, and generating bridging phrases, but is constrained via a factual consistency loss and copy mechanism to stay grounded in the extracted content.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical summarization model that demonstrably improves fluency over extractive-only systems. It will provide a framework for quantifying the trade-off between factual preservation and linguistic quality in hybrid systems, contributing to the broader field of controllable and trustworthy text generation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior hybrid models that often treat extraction and generation as separate or parallel processes, our method explicitly uses the extractive backbone as a verified, immutable scaffold for generation. The 'light generation' is specifically architected as a set of constrained, aspect-aware refinement operations, not free-form rewriting.

proposals.title: Weakly-Supervised Summarization via Uncertainty-Aware Heuristic Alignment and Decomposed Self-Training
proposals.motivation: High-quality abstractive summarization models are heavily reliant on large-scale, human-annotated document-summary pairs, which are expensive and domain-specific to create. This data bottleneck limits the application of summarization to new domains and specialized corpora. Weak supervision from heuristics or unlabeled data offers a promising path to circumvent this need, but existing methods often produce noisy, inconsistent training signals that degrade model performance and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating uncertainty quantification to filter heuristic-generated pseudo-labels and employing a decomposed, aspect-aware self-training framework, we can train robust summarization models that perform comparably to fully-supervised models while requiring minimal or no paired data.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we generate multiple candidate summaries per document using diverse, off-the-shelf heuristics (e.g., lead-3, TextRank, entity-centric extraction). A selector module, inspired by Lapata's work on uncertainty quantification for QA, will estimate the confidence and consensus among these candidates, selecting or fusing them into a refined pseudo-summary only when uncertainty is low. Second, we adapt the decomposed opinion summarization architecture to a self-training loop: an initial model trained on high-confidence pseudo-labels will generate summaries for unlabeled data, which are then verified and refined by the aspect-aware modules before being used for further training, ensuring factual consistency and aspect coverage.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end pipeline for training summarizers with weak supervision that is more robust to noisy labels. It will advance methodology by bridging uncertainty quantification, heuristic merging, and decomposed generation. The resulting models will lower the barrier to deploying summarization in data-scarce domains.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the principled integration of uncertainty estimation directly into the weak supervision pipeline to gate pseudo-label quality, combined with a decomposed self-training mechanism that enforces structured, verifiable summary properties, moving beyond simple consistency-checking or data augmentation.

proposals.title: Pointer Networks for Faithful and Controllable Extractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Abstractive summarization models, while fluent, are prone to generating factual inconsistencies or 'hallucinations,' limiting their reliability in high-stakes domains. Extractive methods, which select salient sentences directly from the source, offer inherent faithfulness but often rely on less flexible, feature-based scoring or simplistic sequence labeling. A method that combines the expressiveness of neural networks with the precision of direct extraction is needed to improve both performance and verifiability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pointer network architecture, trained to directly output a sequence of sentence indices, can outperform traditional extractive methods and rival abstractive models in relevance, while guaranteeing that all summary content is grounded verbatim in the source document.
proposals.method: We will adapt the pointer network mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2015) for document summarization. A bidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder will process the document's sentences. A decoder LSTM, using attention over the encoded sentences, will point to (i.e., select) the index of the next summary sentence at each step. The model will be trained end-to-end using a cross-entropy loss over the pointer vocabulary (the sentence indices) on datasets like CNN/DailyMail. We will explore constrained decoding to prevent repetitions and control summary length.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully neural, end-to-end trainable framework for extractive summarization that is both highly accurate and inherently faithful. It will contribute a new, controllable model for creating verifiable summaries, directly addressing concerns in Mirella Lapata's prior work on uncertainty quantification and verified aspect-aware modules.
proposals.novelty: While pointer networks have been used for tasks like syntactic parsing, their application to directly output a *set* of document indices for summarization is novel, moving beyond word-level copying or sentence scoring to a sequential decision process that learns an optimal extraction order.

proposals.title: Seq2Seq as a Structured Predictor: Conditional Random Fields for End-to-End Information Extraction
proposals.motivation: Information extraction (IE) is crucial for distilling structured knowledge from unstructured text, powering downstream tasks like question answering and summarization. However, many modern IE approaches rely on large generative sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models, which can be computationally heavy and may generate unfaithful or hallucinated outputs. A return to robust, discriminative sequence labeling, enhanced by modern pre-trained representations, offers a promising path for more efficient and reliable extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing extraction as a structured sequence labeling task using a Conditional Random Field (CRF) layer on top of a pre-trained encoder will outperform purely generative seq2seq baselines in accuracy and efficiency, while providing better uncertainty quantification through structured marginal probabilities.
proposals.method: We will adopt a BERT or RoBERTa encoder to generate contextualized token representations. A linear layer will map these to emission scores for entity/relation tags, followed by a CRF layer to model label dependencies and perform globally optimal inference. For complex, multi-sentence extraction (e.g., in opinion summarization), we will explore a hierarchical merging strategy, aggregating sentence-level CRF outputs into a document-level extraction graph. Performance will be evaluated on standard IE benchmarks and uncertainty will be quantified via sequence marginal probabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a strong, efficient baseline for IE that guarantees output structure and improves faithfulness. It will contribute a novel analysis of uncertainty in structured extraction tasks, linking token-level confidence to sequence-level reliability. The method will be validated within the context of downstream applications like verified aspect-aware summarization and retrieval-augmented QA.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are classic, their integration with modern, large-scale pre-trained encoders for end-to-end IE, coupled with a focus on uncertainty quantification derived from the CRF's probabilistic framework, is a non-obvious synthesis of robust old methods with new model capabilities and evaluation concerns.

proposals.title: Controllable Document Expansion via Verified, Aspect-Aware Inverse Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current text generation models often produce generic or unverified content when tasked with expanding a summary into a detailed document. This limits their utility in high-stakes domains like scientific writing, report generation, and knowledge base population, where factual consistency and structured detail are paramount. The inverse of summarization—moving from concise abstraction to rich source text—remains an underexplored yet critical capability for augmenting human information work.
proposals.hypothesis: A decomposed, context-aware generation model, trained with verification modules on paired (summary, document) data, can produce detailed, aspect-faithful document expansions that are more informative, controllable, and factually consistent than standard end-to-end language models.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., T5 or BART) for the expansion task. Inspired by decomposed opinion summarization, we will first segment the input summary into key aspect spans using a learned aspect classifier. For each aspect, a dedicated generator module will produce detailed text, guided by retrieved context from a relevant corpus to ensure factual grounding. A final hierarchical merging module, building on Lapata's prior work, will integrate the aspect-specific expansions into a coherent long-form document, with a verification step to check for hallucination against the retrieved evidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for controllable and verifiable text expansion, advancing beyond generic text continuation. It will contribute a new benchmark dataset for evaluating summary-to-document generation and demonstrate how decomposition and verification, successful in summarization, can be effectively inverted for a complementary generative task.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely frames document expansion as the inverse of verified, aspect-aware summarization, combining decomposition, retrieval-augmentation, and hierarchical merging—techniques proven in Lapata's summarization work—for a novel generative objective, rather than treating it as standard language modeling.

proposals.title: Incremental and Faithful: Real-Time Streaming Summarization with Dynamic Memory and Uncertainty Calibration
proposals.motivation: Current summarization models operate on static, complete documents, failing in real-world scenarios where text arrives as a continuous stream (e.g., live news, meetings, financial reports). This necessitates incremental processing to maintain a coherent, up-to-date summary without costly re-computation of the entire history. Pre-2026, methods for long-context modeling and hierarchical merging exist, but they are not designed for the latency, memory, and faithfulness constraints of true streaming.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a streaming summarizer built upon a dynamic, hierarchical memory structure and equipped with explicit uncertainty quantification can produce coherent, incremental summaries that are more faithful to the evolving input stream than periodic re-summarization of a sliding window.
proposals.method: We will extend the Context-Aware Hierarchical Merging framework to operate incrementally. A transformer-based encoder will process text chunks as they arrive, updating a fixed-size hierarchical memory (sentence- and segment-level representations). A gating mechanism will control information flow into this memory. At each update step, a decoder will generate a summary edit or a new summary sentence, guided by an uncertainty module (inspired by UQ in QA work) that flags when the memory is insufficient for a confident update, triggering a local consolidation. The system will be trained on sequentially presented documents (e.g., streaming news feeds) with a combined loss for summary quality and edit coherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel architecture and training paradigm for real-time, incremental summarization; 2) a publicly available benchmark of streaming text with incremental summary annotations; and 3) an analysis of the trade-offs between latency, coherence, and faithfulness in dynamic summarization, measured by novel metrics for summary stability and factual consistency over time.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of hierarchical merging for streaming—treating the memory as a dynamically updated document representation—and the application of uncertainty quantification to control incremental generation, moving beyond simple sliding-window approaches prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Unconditioned Abstraction: Evaluating the Necessity of Topic Guidance for Extreme Summarization
proposals.motivation: Extreme summarization (e.g., generating single-sentence or headline summaries from long documents) is a critical task for information navigation. Current state-of-the-art approaches, including those using aspect-aware modules or hierarchical merging, often rely on explicit topic or aspect labels to guide content selection and ensure coherence. This raises a fundamental question: are such explicit conditioning signals necessary, or can modern sequence-to-sequence models learn to perform focused abstraction in a completely unconditioned manner?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, with appropriate architectural design and training, a model can perform high-quality extreme summarization without any explicit topic conditioning, and that the resulting summaries will be as coherent and informative as those generated by topic-conditioned models.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained encoder-decoder model (e.g., BART or PEGASUS) for extreme summarization. Crucially, we will remove any explicit topic or aspect conditioning from the input and decoder. To compensate, we will implement a two-stage training regime: first, fine-tuning on a standard summarization dataset (e.g., XSum), and second, training with a contrastive learning objective that rewards summaries preserving the core semantic 'profile' of the source document, measured via sentence embeddings. This encourages the model to implicitly identify and prioritize salient content without topic labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear empirical test of the necessity of explicit topic guidance in abstractive summarization. It will contribute a novel, unconditioned model architecture and training methodology for extreme compression. The findings will inform the design of more flexible and generalizable summarization systems that do not require costly topic annotations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Lapata and others uses verified aspect modules or hierarchical context merging, our approach deliberately strips away explicit topic conditioning to test its fundamental necessity. The novelty lies in using contrastive learning to enforce semantic fidelity as a replacement for explicit topic guidance.

proposals.title: ScaffoldSum: Generating Multi-Perspective Summary Scaffolds to Expose Information Bias and Complexity
proposals.motivation: Personalized summarization systems risk reinforcing filter bubbles by presenting a single, streamlined narrative aligned with a user's inferred preferences. This limits exposure to diverse viewpoints and obscures the inherent complexity and potential bias within source material. A method is needed to structure summaries not for efficiency, but for critical evaluation, promoting media literacy and informed decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured scaffold of multiple, automatically generated narrative summaries, each conditioned on a distinct viewpoint or stakeholder interest derived from the same source documents, will effectively expose contradictions, agreements, and informational gaps, thereby mitigating the filter bubble effect in information consumption.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage pipeline. First, we adapt methods from decomposed opinion summarization to identify key claims and their associated aspects/entities from source documents. Second, we employ a context-aware hierarchical encoder-decoder, similar to Lapata's prior work, but conditioned on distinct 'perspective vectors' representing different stakeholders or epistemic lenses (e.g., economic, environmental). This generates multiple coherent narrative summaries. Finally, we develop a comparative interface that performs cross-summary entailment and contradiction detection to visually map relationships between perspectives, building on recent work in uncertainty quantification for QA.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel summarization paradigm focused on multi-perspective exposition rather than compression. It provides a technical framework for generating and contrasting perspective-conditioned narratives and offers an empirical evaluation of how such scaffolds impact user perception of bias and complexity compared to standard summaries.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty lies in reframing summarization as the generation of a comparative scaffold of narratives, moving beyond single-summary or multi-document fusion. This integrates viewpoint conditioning, claim verification, and comparative analysis into a unified system for bias mitigation, a non-obvious synthesis of existing methods for a new objective.

proposals.title: Conceptual Compressive Sensing for Query-Conditioned Extreme Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current summarization and retrieval-augmented QA systems often produce generic, one-size-fits-all outputs, failing to adapt to diverse user intents and conceptual interests. This inefficiency is pronounced in long-document settings, where users need rapid access to specific facets of information. Drawing an analogy from signal processing, we posit that a user's query defines a 'sparsity basis' in which the relevant information is concentrated, enabling far more efficient and personalized information extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a user's information need as a conceptual sparsity basis, we can apply principles from compressive sensing to design an adaptive system that reconstructs a personalized summary from a minimal, query-conditioned set of document 'measurements'.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage neural architecture. First, a context-aware encoder will infer a user-specific sparsity basis from the query and document context, parameterized as a transformation matrix for the document's token embeddings. Second, a sparse coding layer, regularized via an L1 penalty, will select a minimal set of basis-aligned embeddings as 'measurements.' A transformer-based decoder, using cross-attention over these selected embeddings, will reconstruct the final personalized summary. Evaluation will leverage datasets like QMSum and ELI5, measuring summary quality and the system's efficiency via the measurement compression ratio.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel framework for extreme, query-specific summarization that explicitly optimizes for informational efficiency, moving beyond fixed-length extraction. It will provide new methods for uncertainty-aware, adaptive information filtering in retrieval-augmented systems, directly extending Dr. Lapata's work on uncertainty quantification and hierarchical merging.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal translation of compressive sensing theory—sparse signal recovery—to the NLP task of personalized summarization, where the 'sparsity basis' is dynamically inferred from user context rather than being a fixed, known transform.

proposals.title: Calibrating Confidence: A Claim-Driven, Uncertainty-Aware Framework for Validating Summary Evaluation Metrics
proposals.motivation: Automatic metrics for evaluating factual consistency and grounding in text summarization are proliferating, but their reliability is poorly understood. They often correlate poorly with human judgment on specific, challenging claims, and their scores lack interpretable confidence intervals. This creates a reproducibility crisis where metric performance is not a validated measurement but an opaque, context-dependent signal.
proposals.hypothesis: By decomposing summaries into verifiable claims and using human judgments on a strategically selected subset—identified via uncertainty estimation—as calibration data, we can build a transparent framework that validates, weights, and explains the outputs of automatic metrics, quantifying their reliability in specific contexts.
proposals.method: We will first apply claim decomposition models (e.g., based on factuality or aspect-aware extraction) to source-summary pairs. For each claim, we will compute a suite of automatic metric scores (e.g., NLI-based, QA-based, entailment scores) and an epistemic uncertainty estimate using methods like Monte Carlo dropout or ensemble variance. Claims with high uncertainty will be prioritized for efficient human annotation on factual consistency. These human judgments will then train a calibration model (e.g., a Bayesian logistic regression or a meta-learner) that maps and weighs the raw metric scores to calibrated confidence estimates, providing explainable error bars and context-specific reliability scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated evaluation toolkit that outputs not just a score but a confidence interval and a reliability diagnosis for automatic summary assessment. It will provide a principled methodology for anchoring metrics to human judgment efficiently and offer new insights into which metric families are trustworthy for different types of factual errors.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses human judgments as a static gold standard for benchmarking, we treat them as dynamic calibration data to build a meta-evaluation framework. The integration of uncertainty quantification from Lapata's prior research to drive efficient human-in-the-loop calibration for metric validation is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: A Causal Psychometric Framework for Summary Evaluation: Testing the Sufficiency of Automatic Metrics via Structural Equation Modeling
proposals.motivation: The field of abstractive summarization lacks a principled, evidence-based understanding of what automatic evaluation metrics actually measure. Current practice treats metrics like ROUGE, BERTScore, or QA-based scores as direct, sufficient proxies for complex summary qualities like factual consistency and grounding. This assumption is untested and potentially flawed, leading to unreliable system comparisons and misguided research directions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core summary qualities are latent constructs that cannot be sufficiently captured by any single existing metric, but that their causal relationships with a battery of metrics and human judgments can be formally modeled. The statistical fit of such a model will provide a rigorous test of whether current automatic metrics constitute a sufficient measurement system.
proposals.method: We will adapt Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) from psychometrics. First, we will define latent constructs (e.g., Factual Consistency, Coherence, Grounding) and specify a causal measurement model linking them to observed variables: a suite of automatic metrics (e.g., BERTScore, SummaC, QAEval, NLI-based entailment scores) and targeted human judgments. Using established SEM software (e.g., lavaan in R), we will fit the model to data from diverse summarization datasets (e.g., XSum, CNN/DM). Model fit indices (CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) will quantitatively test if the constellation of metrics sufficiently explains the latent constructs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, causal framework for diagnosing the validity of automatic summary evaluation. It will produce concrete, statistical evidence for or against the sufficiency of current metrics, moving beyond anecdotal criticism. The resulting framework will offer researchers a principled methodology for selecting and combining metrics based on their relationship to underlying qualities of interest.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual analogy and technical adaptation of psychometric causal modeling (SEM) to NLP evaluation, a significant departure from the standard approach of proposing new metrics or correlating with human scores. It reframes the evaluation problem from one of correlation to one of causal measurement theory and latent construct validation.

proposals.title: Factual Grounding and Consistency: A Modular, Verifiable Framework for Evaluating Abstractive Summaries
proposals.motivation: Current automatic metrics for abstractive summarization (e.g., ROUGE, BERTScore) primarily assess surface-level lexical or semantic similarity to references, failing to reliably measure factual consistency with the source or grounding in verifiable evidence. This misalignment with human judgment undermines trust in model outputs and hinders progress in high-stakes applications. A robust, automatic evaluation framework focused on factual integrity is a critical unmet need in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular evaluation framework that decomposes summary evaluation into verifiable sub-tasks—specifically factual grounding and consistency—can provide a more reliable and interpretable automatic assessment than existing holistic metrics, correlating better with human judgments.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, model-based framework. First, a **fact extraction and alignment module** decomposes the source and summary into atomic factual claims (e.g., using OpenIE or fine-tuned sequence taggers). Second, a **verification module** assesses each summary claim for grounding and consistency. This leverages retrieval-augmented question answering (inspired by Lapata's QA work) to query the source for evidence, and natural language inference (NLI) models for entailment/contradiction checking. The final score aggregates verifiable outcomes, incorporating uncertainty estimates for model confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source evaluation toolkit that provides fine-grained, interpretable diagnostics of factual errors in summaries. It will establish a new benchmark for factual consistency evaluation, moving beyond n-gram overlap. The framework's modular design, inspired by decomposed summarization methods, allows for targeted improvements in individual components (e.g., claim extraction, verification).
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats factual consistency as a monolithic classification task, our framework decomposes it into verifiable, aspect-aware sub-problems, integrating retrieval-based evidence checking and uncertainty quantification—a synthesis not present in pre-2026 metrics like SummaC or FactCC.

proposals.title: Knowledge-Grounded Abstractive Summarization via Verified Graph Retrieval and Fusion
proposals.motivation: Abstractive summarization models often generate factually inconsistent or incomplete summaries due to reliance on parametric knowledge and source text alone. Integrating structured external knowledge, such as knowledge bases or semantic graphs, can provide verified factual grounding and improve coverage of key entities and relations. This addresses a core limitation in pre-2026 summarization, where factual hallucination remains a critical barrier to reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a retrieval-augmented summarization model, which dynamically retrieves and fuses verified subgraphs from structured knowledge sources conditioned on the input document, will generate summaries with higher factual consistency and informativeness than models relying solely on parametric knowledge or unstructured retrieval.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture building on Dr. Lapata's prior work. First, a context-aware retriever (inspired by her QA uncertainty work) will query a knowledge graph (e.g., Wikidata) to fetch relevant entity-centric subgraphs, with a learned module to quantify retrieval confidence. Second, a hierarchical encoder-decoder model (extending her context-aware merging) will fuse the document representation with the retrieved graph via a graph attention mechanism. The decoder will be constrained by verified aspects (leveraging her decomposed opinion summarization) to promote factual grounding in the generated summary.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for knowledge-grounded summarization with explicit uncertainty modeling for retrieved facts, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating factual consistency in summaries against knowledge graphs, and 3) empirical evidence on the benefits of structured versus unstructured knowledge integration for abstractive summarization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit verification and uncertainty-aware fusion of structured knowledge subgraphs, moving beyond simple entity linking or appending triples. This combines Dr. Lapata's expertise in verified modules, hierarchical merging, and uncertainty quantification into a unified approach for knowledge-enhanced generation.

proposals.title: Conceptually Anchored Topic Modeling: Regularizing Neural Topic Models with External Knowledge for Interpretable and Stable Discovery
proposals.motivation: Unsupervised topic models like Neural Topic Models (NTMs) often produce semantically unstable or incoherent topics due to purely statistical optimization, limiting their practical utility in downstream tasks like summarization or question answering. While methods exist to incorporate seed words or metadata, they require manual input or pre-defined tags, which is not scalable. A method is needed to ground topics in external knowledge automatically, ensuring interpretability without sacrificing the data-driven nature of discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that weakly regularizing a neural topic model with automatically extracted conceptual anchors from knowledge bases (e.g., Wikipedia, ConceptNet) will yield topics that are both coherent and interpretably aligned with human-understandable concepts, thereby preventing semantic drift and improving performance in document understanding tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend a base NTM (e.g., a ProdLDA or ETM variant) with a novel regularization term. First, we will automatically gather conceptual anchors for potential topics by retrieving relevant entities and definitions from knowledge sources using the model's initial topic-word distributions as queries. Then, we will design a regularization objective, such as a contrastive loss or a KL-divergence penalty, that pulls the learned topic representations closer to the embeddings of their assigned anchors while maintaining the model's generative capability. This approach mirrors the 'context-aware merging' and 'verified aspect-aware modules' from Lapata's prior work, applying verification and grounding principles to unsupervised discovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid topic modeling framework that balances data-driven discovery with knowledge-based grounding. It will provide a robust, interpretable topic representation method that can be reliably integrated into downstream NLP pipelines, such as the retrieval-augmented QA and summarization systems central to Lapata's research. We will also release a benchmark for evaluating topic stability and conceptual coherence.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the automated, weakly-supervised anchoring mechanism, which regularizes an entirely unsupervised model without requiring human-curated seeds or altering its core architecture, a non-obvious fusion of knowledge grounding and statistical topic modeling.

proposals.title: Gradient-Based Topic Discovery: An Ecological Niche Modeling Approach to Unsupervised Document Representation
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised topic models (e.g., LDA, NMF) impose a discrete, bag-of-words structure, often failing to capture the continuous, overlapping semantic gradients that characterize real-world discourse. This limits their interpretability and utility for downstream tasks like nuanced question answering and summarization, where understanding the underlying semantic landscape is crucial. A method that discovers these fundamental, continuous dimensions directly from co-occurrence patterns would provide a more natural and powerful representation of document content.
proposals.hypothesis: By analogizing documents to ecological sites and words to species, the continuous latent dimensions (topic niches) that structure a corpus can be discovered through gradient-based ordination techniques from ecology, yielding more interpretable and semantically-grounded representations than discrete topic models.
proposals.method: We will formalize the analogy: documents are sites, term frequencies are species abundances, and latent semantic dimensions are environmental gradients. We adapt Non-metric Multidimensional Scaling (NMDS) and Detrended Correspondence Analysis (DCA) to operate on document-term matrices, optimizing for a low-dimensional embedding where term co-occurrence distances are preserved. This core gradient discovery will be integrated with a neural module (e.g., a lightweight MLP) to project new documents into the discovered continuous topic space, enabling scalable inference. Evaluation will use intrinsic metrics (e.g., coherence of gradient extremes) and extrinsic performance on Lapata's established tasks like context-aware summarization and retrieval-augmented QA.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for continuous, gradient-based topic discovery, moving beyond discrete clusters. It will provide a new tool for analyzing semantic structure in large corpora, with direct applications to improving the contextual grounding and uncertainty quantification in retrieval-augmented systems. The ecological analogy offers a principled, interpretable foundation for modeling semantic overlap and drift.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct transfer of ecological gradient analysis techniques (ordination) to unsupervised NLP, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary analogy. Unlike neural topic models that still often output discrete mixtures, this method explicitly seeks to discover the continuous axes of semantic variation that define 'topic niches'.

proposals.title: Ranking by Narrative Coherence: A Neural Topic Flow Model for Document Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Current neural ranking models, while effective, often treat documents as bags of sentences or rely on static global topic representations, neglecting the sequential, narrative structure that makes a document coherent. This is a critical gap, as a document's thematic flow and logical progression are key to its understandability and utility for a user's information need. Evaluating and rewarding this latent coherence could significantly improve retrieval quality, especially for complex queries requiring synthesis of information from long-form documents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling a document's latent, evolving topic structure—its 'topic flow'—and using this representation to score inter-sentence thematic coherence will produce a ranking function that better identifies high-quality, logically structured documents compared to models based on independent sentence or static topic assumptions.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical neural architecture. A lower-level transformer encoder (e.g., BERT) processes individual sentences, while a higher-level recurrent module (e.g., a GRU or Transformer) models the sequence of sentence representations. This recurrent layer will be regularized via a neural topic model (e.g., a Neural Variational Document Model) to infer a smooth, latent topic trajectory. The ranking score will combine traditional relevance signals (from CLS tokens) with a novel coherence score derived from the predictability and smoothness of this inferred topic flow, using techniques from Lapata's prior work on context-aware hierarchical merging and uncertainty quantification.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, end-to-end trainable neural ranking model that jointly optimizes for relevance and narrative coherence, 2) a new interpretable metric for document coherence based on latent topic dynamics, and 3) an evaluation framework and benchmark for assessing coherence-aware retrieval, advancing beyond purely lexical or semantic matching.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a dynamic, latent topic flow model into the ranking objective, moving beyond treating sentences as independent or using pre-defined, static topics. This approach fundamentally shifts ranking from assessing static content to evaluating the quality of information presentation.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Hierarchical Summarization via Verified Extractive-Abstractive Fusion
proposals.motivation: While hybrid extractive-abstractive summarization promises to combine factual grounding with fluent generation, existing pipelines often propagate errors from the extractive stage and lack mechanisms to quantify confidence in the selected content. This is critical for long-document and opinion summarization, where key information is dispersed and verifying aspect coverage is essential for trustworthy outputs. Grounding abstractive generation on uncertain extracts can lead to hallucinated or incomplete summaries.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage model that first performs uncertainty-quantified extraction and then conditions abstractive generation on both the extracted content and its associated confidence scores will produce more faithful, informative, and controllable summaries than standard hybrid or end-to-end abstractive models.
proposals.method: We propose a context-aware hierarchical merging framework. First, an extractor module with built-in uncertainty quantification (e.g., via Monte Carlo dropout or ensemble methods) selects and scores salient sentences or spans. Second, these verified extracts, along with their confidence scores, are fed as soft constraints into a transformer-based abstractive generator, using a mechanism akin to 'certainty gates' to modulate attention. The model will be trained end-to-end, with the extractor's uncertainty signal providing a learning signal for the generator, inspired by decomposed and verified training from opinion summarization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, trainable architecture for hybrid summarization that explicitly models and utilizes uncertainty, advancing beyond simple pipeline approaches. It will provide new benchmarks for faithfulness and informativeness in long-document and multi-aspect opinion summarization, directly extending Dr. Lapata's recent work on uncertainty in QA and verified modules.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, trainable integration of uncertainty quantification from the extractive stage into the abstractive generation process, moving beyond using extracts as mere hard input. This creates a self-verifying loop where the generator learns to rely on high-confidence content and compensate for or question low-confidence extracts.

proposals.title: Policy Optimization via Natural Evolution Strategies for Non-Differentiable Summary Quality
proposals.motivation: State-of-the-art abstractive summarization models are trained via maximum likelihood or policy gradient reinforcement learning (RL). However, RL policy gradients for text generation suffer from high variance and instability when optimizing for complex, non-differentiable metrics like ROUGE or readability scores. This creates a need for more robust optimization methods that can directly navigate these discrete output spaces without relying on differentiable reward estimators.
proposals.hypothesis: A Natural Evolution Strategy (NES) applied to the parameters of a summarization policy network will provide a more stable and effective optimization pathway for non-differentiable quality metrics than standard policy gradient methods like REINFORCE.
proposals.method: We will implement a black-box optimizer where a population of parameter vectors is sampled by adding Gaussian noise to the weights of a pre-trained transformer-based summarization policy (e.g., BART or PEGASUS). Each perturbed policy generates summaries for a batch of documents, which are then scored by a composite fitness function combining RUGE-L, BERTScore, and a readability metric. The central policy's parameters are updated by a weighted average of the noise vectors, with weights proportional to the normalized fitness scores, following the NES gradient estimator. This process iteratively evolves the policy toward higher-scoring regions of the parameter space.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, gradient-free optimization framework for text generation that is more robust to reward sparsity and variance than standard RL. It will provide empirical evidence on benchmark datasets (e.g., CNN/DailyMail, XSum) for the comparative stability and performance of ES-based training, potentially establishing a new alternative for direct metric optimization in NLP.
proposals.novelty: While ES has been explored in continuous control, its application to direct optimization of neural text generation policies against holistic, non-differentiable metrics is novel. This approach circumvents the need for a differentiable reward proxy or complex baseline estimators required in typical RL fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Policy Optimization for Non-Differentiable Objectives in Abstractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current abstractive summarization models are typically trained via maximum likelihood estimation, which optimizes for token-level prediction but is misaligned with end-task evaluation metrics like ROUGE or human-assessed readability. This discrepancy leads to a known performance ceiling where models generate fluent but suboptimal summaries according to these critical, non-differentiable metrics. Directly optimizing for these metrics is a core challenge in generating high-quality, task-aligned summaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing summarization as a reinforcement learning problem, where the policy network is a fine-tuned language model and the reward is shaped by a combination of ROUGE and learned readability scores, will yield summaries that significantly outperform those from standard supervised learning on these target metrics.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., BART or PEGASUS) as the initial policy. The model will be fine-tuned using a policy gradient method, specifically REINFORCE with a baseline, to maximize a composite reward function. This reward will combine standard ROUGE scores with a learned readability estimator trained on pre-2026 datasets like SummEval. To stabilize training and incorporate task structure, we will explore a hierarchical action space, decomposing the summary generation into a high-level content selection policy and a low-level phrasing policy, informed by Dr. Lapata's prior work on hierarchical merging and decomposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust RL-based framework for direct metric optimization in summarization, demonstrating measurable gains in ROUGE and human-centric readability scores. It will also provide analysis into the trade-offs between fluency, faithfulness, and optimized scores, contributing a new methodology for aligning NLG models with complex, non-differentiable objectives.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been applied to ROUGE optimization before, the novelty lies in the integration of a learned readability component into the reward and the application of a context-aware, hierarchical policy structure specifically for long-form summarization, moving beyond simple token-level rewards.

proposals.title: Navigable Topic Maps for Controllable Summarization via Topographic Variational Autoencoders
proposals.motivation: Current neural summarizers offer limited fine-grained control over thematic focus, often producing generic or unfocused summaries. While conditioning on discrete topics or keywords exists, it lacks the ability to navigate a continuous, semantically structured space of themes. Drawing inspiration from cognitive maps in neuroscience, we propose that a structured, continuous latent topic space would enable more precise and interpretable control over summary content, a key challenge in controllable text generation.
proposals.hypothesis: A topographic VAE can learn a latent topic space where geometry reflects semantic relationships, and conditioning a summarizer on coordinates within this space will allow for smooth, interpretable control over the thematic focus of generated summaries.
proposals.method: We will train a Topographic VAE (TVAE) with a von Mises-Fisher prior to project document representations onto a structured latent hypersphere, enforcing neighborhood preservation. This creates a smooth 'topic map' where nearby points share semantics. A transformer-based summarizer (e.g., BART or T5) will be conditioned on these latent coordinates via cross-attention. We will evaluate on long-document datasets (e.g., arXiv, GovReport), controlling summaries by selecting or interpolating points on the learned manifold.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for controllable summarization via a navigable latent topic geometry. It will provide a method for generating aspect-specific summaries with fine-grained control and offer an interpretable visualization of the document's thematic landscape, linking to the researcher's interests in context-aware merging and verified aspect-aware modules.
proposals.novelty: The adaptation of topographic VAEs—used in computational neuroscience for cognitive maps—to create structured, continuous topic spaces for text generation control is novel. This moves beyond discrete or unstructured latent spaces, offering a geometrically meaningful interface for conditioning.

proposals.title: Adversarial Realism Training for Faithful and Coherent Abstractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in abstractive summarization, models often produce factually inconsistent or stylistically unnatural text, limiting their trustworthiness and practical utility. Ensuring that generated summaries are both faithful to the source and indistinguishable from human-written prose remains a core, unsolved challenge in NLP. This work directly addresses the realism gap, a critical barrier to deployment in high-stakes domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a fine-grained, multi-discriminator adversarial objective into the training of a summarization model will significantly improve the factual faithfulness and stylistic realism of its outputs without sacrificing informativeness.
proposals.method: We will extend a Transformer-based encoder-decoder summarization model (e.g., BART or PEGASUS) with an adversarial training framework. The generator will be trained against a suite of discriminators: one for overall document-summary realism (trained on human vs. model summaries), and specialized verifiers for factual consistency, inspired by recent work on uncertainty quantification in QA. These verifiers will use decomposed, aspect-aware attention to cross-check claims in the summary against the source document, providing a fine-grained adversarial signal.
proposals.contribution: This research will yield a novel adversarial training paradigm for summarization that explicitly optimizes for both global realism and local factual integrity. We will provide a new, publicly available benchmark for evaluating summary realism and faithfulness, and a model that sets a new state-of-the-art in producing human-quality, reliable summaries.
proposals.novelty: While GANs have been explored in text generation, their application to summarization has been limited to basic realism. Our novelty lies in the integration of decomposed, fact-verifying discriminators—building on Lapata's recent work in verified aspect-aware modules and uncertainty quantification—into the adversarial objective, creating a multi-faceted 'realism' signal.

proposals.title: Reverse Summarization: Generating Hierarchical Outlines from Single-Sentence Summaries
proposals.motivation: While summarization condenses documents, the reverse task of generating a plausible document structure from a brief summary is an underexplored frontier. This capability is crucial for creative writing aids, educational tools, and planning long-form content, addressing a core gap in document generation. It also serves as a novel probe for a model's understanding of narrative and argumentative coherence beyond surface-level text.
proposals.hypothesis: A model can generate a coherent, multi-level document outline from a single-sentence summary by decomposing the summary's core claim into constituent topics and sub-topics, and that this process can be guided and improved by explicit uncertainty quantification over the generated structure.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, context-aware hierarchical model. First, a verifier module, inspired by decomposed opinion summarization, will extract and verify key aspects (entities, claims, themes) from the input summary. Second, a hierarchical decoder, building on context-aware merging techniques, will generate a tree-structured outline, where each node is a key point. We will adapt uncertainty quantification methods from retrieval-augmented QA to score the confidence of each branch and prune low-confidence sub-structures, ensuring robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce and formalize the novel task of 'reverse summarization,' providing a benchmark dataset and evaluation metrics. It will contribute a new model architecture that integrates verification, hierarchical generation, and uncertainty-aware refinement, advancing methods for controllable and interpretable long-form text generation.
proposals.novelty: The proposal reverses the established summarization paradigm, focusing on structure generation from a summary rather than summary generation from a document. It uniquely combines Mirella Lapata's recent themes—verified decomposition, hierarchical merging, and uncertainty quantification—into a single, novel framework for this new task.

proposals.title: Extreme Summarization for Micro-Content: A Decomposed, Uncertainty-Aware Approach to Very Short Documents
proposals.motivation: Current summarization research focuses on long documents, but the proliferation of micro-content like social media posts and news alerts presents a distinct challenge. Summarizing these very short texts requires extreme compression, where the goal is not to reduce length but to rephrase or highlight the core informational or affective signal without losing fidelity. This is crucial for downstream tasks like real-time alert aggregation and social media monitoring, where processing speed and accuracy are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decomposed, aspect-aware summarization framework, enhanced with explicit uncertainty quantification, can outperform standard sequence-to-sequence models on very short documents by better managing information sparsity and preventing hallucination.
proposals.method: We will adapt the decomposed opinion summarization framework from Lapata's prior work, treating micro-content as a collection of latent aspects (e.g., event, sentiment, entity). For each input, a verification module will assess the presence and confidence of these aspects. The summarizer will then be conditioned on this verified, structured representation. We will integrate uncertainty quantification techniques from Lapata's QA work, using Monte Carlo dropout or ensemble methods during generation to flag low-confidence summaries for very ambiguous inputs. Evaluation will use a new dataset of tweet threads and news alerts paired with human-written 'headline' summaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rigorous benchmark and methodology for extreme summarization of micro-content. It will contribute a novel model that explicitly handles information sparsity and uncertainty, advancing the field beyond simple truncation or extraction. The resulting framework will provide more reliable, interpretable summaries for high-velocity information streams.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a decomposed, verification-driven architecture—typically used for long-form opinion summarization—to the inverse problem of very short texts, and in explicitly modeling generation uncertainty as a core component of the summarization process for this domain.

proposals.title: Multi-Target Text Simplification via Proficiency-Aware Decomposition and Uncertainty-Guided Generation
proposals.motivation: Current text simplification models are typically trained and evaluated against a single 'simple' reference, ignoring the inherent diversity of valid simplifications and the varied proficiency levels of readers (e.g., language learners vs. individuals with cognitive impairments). This single-reference assumption limits the utility and personalization of simplification systems. The problem is exacerbated in complex, information-dense domains where different readers require different types of lexical, syntactic, or discourse-level adjustments.
proposals.hypothesis: A model that explicitly conditions on a target reader proficiency level and decomposes the simplification task into verified, aspect-aware operations (e.g., lexical substitution, sentence splitting, discourse linking) will generate multiple, high-quality, and proficiency-appropriate simplifications more effectively than a single-reference, end-to-end model.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular, encoder-decoder architecture. First, a context-aware hierarchical encoder will represent the source document. Second, drawing from decomposed summarization methods, we will design verified, trainable modules for specific simplification operations (lexical, structural, elaborative). These modules will be guided by a proficiency embedding (e.g., CEFR level A1-C2). Third, we will incorporate uncertainty quantification techniques from retrieval-augmented QA to model the distribution over possible valid simplifications at each step, enabling the generation of multiple diverse outputs. Training will use multi-reference datasets (e.g., TurkCorpus, Newsela) with proficiency-level annotations derived from readability metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel framework for controllable, multi-target text simplification, 2) a new dataset with proficiency-level annotations for evaluation, and 3) a systematic analysis of how simplification strategies should vary with reader proficiency. It advances the field from single-output generation to personalized, uncertainty-aware language adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of proficiency-aware control and uncertainty-guided generation within a decomposed, verifiable architecture for simplification—a departure from standard sequence-to-sequence models. This combines Mirella Lapata's expertise in decomposed, verified modules and uncertainty quantification for a new, personalized NLG task.

proposals.title: Beyond Lexical Simplicity: A Framework for Evaluating and Generating Structurally Clear Text
proposals.motivation: Current text simplification research and systems predominantly equate simplicity with lexical and syntactic reduction, measured by surface metrics like sentence length and vocabulary grade level. However, this can inadvertently obscure logical flow, causal relationships, and discourse structure, making text harder to comprehend for both humans and downstream NLP tasks like question answering. A paradigm shift is needed to define and operationalize clarity in terms of logical and structural transparency, moving beyond mere word substitution.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling and optimizing for logical and structural clarity—through decomposable discourse graphs and verified information ordering—will produce text that is more comprehensible and useful for reasoning than text optimized solely for lexical simplicity.
proposals.method: We will develop a new evaluation framework and generation model. First, we will define metrics for structural clarity, building on Rhetorical Structure Theory and dependency graph depth, and create a dataset by annotating simplified texts for logical coherence. Second, we will adapt a controllable text generation model (e.g., based on T5 or BART) using a decomposed, aspect-aware approach inspired by Lapata's recent work. The model will take as input a complexity profile specifying targets for lexical simplicity and structural clarity, using a hierarchical merging mechanism to integrate these constraints during the decoding process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel benchmark and metrics for evaluating structural clarity in simplified text, 2) A controllable generation model that can prioritize logical transparency over lexical reduction, and 3) Empirical evidence, via human and task-based evaluation (e.g., QA performance), that structural clarity is a critical and distinct dimension of comprehensibility.
proposals.novelty: It challenges the core objective of lexical simplification as an end goal, proposing a new operationalization of clarity. The method uniquely combines discourse-aware evaluation with a controllable generation architecture that treats structural and lexical features as separate, composable constraints.

proposals.title: Controllable Text Simplification via Continuous Depth Modulation and Uncertainty-Aware Verification
proposals.motivation: Current text simplification models produce a single, often arbitrary, level of simplification, failing to adapt to diverse user needs (e.g., language learners vs. individuals with cognitive impairments). This lack of granular, user-specified control limits the practical utility of simplification systems in real-world applications. A model that can reliably scale its output to a target simplicity level would enable personalized accessibility and content adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: A text simplification model, conditioned on a continuous simplicity depth parameter and augmented with uncertainty quantification for its lexical and syntactic operations, can generate outputs that are both faithful to the source and reliably adhere to user-specified simplicity targets.
proposals.method: We will build upon a sequence-to-sequence architecture (e.g., T5 or BART) by integrating a continuous depth embedding as a conditioning signal, similar to control codes for style. To ensure reliable depth control, we will develop a hierarchical merging mechanism, inspired by Lapata's prior work, that decomposes complex sentences and selectively merges simplified clauses based on the target depth. We will incorporate an uncertainty quantification module, akin to those used in retrieval-augmented QA, to verify the appropriateness of lexical substitutions and structural changes at each depth level, allowing the model to flag or adjust low-confidence simplifications.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, user-controllable simplification model with a verifiable output mechanism. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating depth-aware simplification and advance methods for integrating explicit control and uncertainty quantification into generative text-to-text tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work controls discrete attributes (e.g., formality), fine-grained, continuous control over simplification depth is unexplored. The integration of uncertainty-aware verification for controllable generation is a novel combination, moving beyond standard conditional training to produce more reliable and interpretable outputs.

proposals.title: Verifiable and Controllable Natural Language Generation via Logical Form Inversion
proposals.motivation: Current sequence-to-sequence models for tasks like question answering and summarization often produce outputs that are difficult to verify or control, leading to issues with factual consistency and hallucination. Inverting the paradigm by using a logical form as the input to generate natural language offers a path to more interpretable and grounded generation. This aligns with the growing pre-2026 need for reliable, explainable NLP systems, particularly in high-stakes domains.
proposals.hypothesis: By conditioning a generative model on a formal, structured logical representation, we can produce more faithful, controllable, and verifiable natural language utterances than standard end-to-end generation. This structured-to-text process will improve performance on tasks requiring strict adherence to input semantics.
proposals.method: We will develop a context-aware, hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder will process a logical form (e.g., AMR, DRS, or a task-specific formalism) into a structured latent representation. The decoder, inspired by decomposed summarization methods, will generate the natural language output in a verified, stepwise manner, potentially using aspect-aware modules to handle different semantic components. We will leverage uncertainty quantification techniques from retrieval-augmented QA to assess the confidence of each generation step relative to the input logic.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for verifiable text generation, directly linking formal semantics to fluent output. It will provide new benchmarks and evaluation metrics for faithfulness in generation tasks. The methodology will advance controllable NLP, enabling systems where the reasoning trace (logical form) is a core, inspectable component.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses logical forms as *targets* for semantic parsing, we invert this to use them as *source* controls for generation, integrating verification modules. This moves beyond template-based realization by employing modern neural architectures to ensure fluency while maintaining strict semantic fidelity.
