proposals.title: Retrieval-Augmented Neural Parsing with Uncertainty-Aware Knowledge Integration
proposals.motivation: Neural parsers, while powerful, often struggle with rare or domain-specific linguistic constructions due to limitations in their training data. Integrating structured external knowledge, such as grammatical lexicons or semantic ontologies, could provide the necessary grounding to improve accuracy and robustness. This addresses a core challenge in parsing, aligning with the broader pre-2026 NLP trend of augmenting models with external knowledge to overcome data sparsity and improve generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically retrieving and integrating relevant knowledge from structured linguistic resources into a neural parser, with explicit modeling of retrieval uncertainty, will lead to significant improvements in parsing accuracy, particularly for low-frequency syntactic and semantic phenomena.
proposals.method: We will develop a retrieval-augmented parsing architecture where a base neural parser (e.g., a transition-based or graph-based model) is equipped with a retriever module. This module will query structured knowledge bases (e.g., VerbNet, FrameNet, or a grammar rule lexicon) based on the current parsing state and input sentence. Inspired by Lapata's recent work on uncertainty quantification in QA, we will model the confidence of the retrieved knowledge using a latent variable. A hierarchical merging module, similar to her context-aware merging for summarization, will then fuse the parser's internal representations with the verified, uncertainty-weighted external knowledge to guide the final parsing decisions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, modular framework for knowledge-augmented syntactic and semantic parsing that explicitly handles the reliability of external sources. It will advance the field by providing a principled method to make neural parsers more data-efficient and linguistically informed, with empirical gains demonstrated on benchmarks like SPMRL and the English Web Treebank.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the application of uncertainty-aware retrieval augmentation—a technique explored in QA—to the structured prediction task of parsing, combined with a hierarchical merging strategy for knowledge integration, moving beyond simple concatenation or attention-based fusion used in prior work.

proposals.title: Memory-Augmented Semantic Parsing for World Knowledge-Intensive Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Semantic parsing for question answering often fails on queries requiring world knowledge not present in the input text, relying on static, parametric knowledge within large language models which can be incomplete or outdated. This limitation is critical for applications like open-domain QA and complex reasoning, where factual accuracy and temporal relevance are paramount. A dynamic, updatable memory store for facts, distinct from model parameters, could provide a more reliable and inspectable knowledge source.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a semantic parsing model with a differentiable, key-value memory network for explicit fact storage and retrieval will improve the accuracy and robustness of knowledge-intensive question answering, particularly for queries involving rare or temporally scoped facts.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture: a semantic parser (e.g., a seq2seq Transformer) generates a draft logical form, while a neural memory module, implemented as a differentiable key-value memory network, is queried in parallel using the question and draft parse as keys. Retrieved facts are fused with the parser's hidden states via a gated attention mechanism, guiding a refinement step for the final parse. The memory is pre-populated from knowledge graphs (e.g., Wikidata) and can be updated without retraining the full model, with retrieval trained end-to-end using a QA loss on datasets like MetaQA or WebQuestionsSP.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, modular framework for integrating non-parametric world knowledge into semantic parsing, improving performance on knowledge-bound queries. It will also provide new insights into the separation of reasoning (parsing) and factual recall, enhancing model interpretability and facilitating knowledge updates.
proposals.novelty: While memory networks have been used for QA over text, their integration into the *semantic parsing* process to dynamically retrieve and apply structured world facts during parse generation is novel. This moves beyond using memory as a simple retriever to making it an integral, guiding component of the structured prediction task.

proposals.title: Unified Semantic Parsing via Multi-Domain Pre-Training on Heterogeneous Query-LF Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current semantic parsing models are typically trained on single-domain datasets, limiting their ability to generalize across diverse applications and real-world query distributions. Scaling to a massive, heterogeneous corpus of queries paired with logical forms (LFs) from domains like databases, APIs, and knowledge graphs could enable a single, robust model capable of handling varied user intents and compositional reasoning. This addresses a key bottleneck in creating practical, open-domain question answering and task-oriented dialogue systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training a sequence-to-sequence transformer on a massive, multi-domain corpus of (query, logical form) pairs will yield a model with superior zero-shot and few-shot generalization to unseen domains and novel logical form schemas, compared to models trained on individual or concatenated single-domain datasets.
proposals.method: We will construct a heterogeneous corpus by merging existing datasets (e.g., Spider, WikiSQL, Overnight, MTOP, CoSQL) and synthetically augmenting them. The core model will be a T5 or BART-style encoder-decoder. We will adapt the context-aware hierarchical merging technique from Lapata's summarization work to fuse information from multiple, potentially noisy, parallel data sources during pre-training. Training will involve a denoising objective and a novel consistency loss that leverages uncertainty quantification methods to weight examples from different domains based on their inferred reliability, ensuring robust learning from the heterogeneous mix.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new, large-scale benchmark for cross-domain semantic parsing evaluation; 2) a pre-trained model that sets a new state-of-the-art for zero-shot parsing; and 3) methodological insights into effectively merging and training on heterogeneous structured-output data, advancing the field beyond single-domain or single-task paradigms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic scaling to massive *heterogeneity* in logical form schemas, not just dataset size, and the application of Lapata's context-aware merging and uncertainty quantification techniques—previously used for summarization and QA—to the distinct challenge of multi-domain semantic parsing pre-training.

proposals.title: Syntactic Parsing for Cross-Domain Data Augmentation in Opinion Summarization
proposals.motivation: Neural opinion summarization models require large amounts of domain-specific training data, which is costly and time-consuming to create. This data scarcity limits the application of high-performance summarizers to new domains, such as new product categories or emerging social media platforms. An automated, parser-driven data augmentation method could overcome this bottleneck, enabling rapid adaptation without extensive human annotation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a constituency or dependency parser can be used to generate linguistically valid, domain-adapted training data by performing controlled syntactic transformations on existing opinionated text, and that models trained on this augmented data will achieve superior performance on new domains compared to standard transfer learning baselines.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained neural parser (e.g., from the Stanford CoreNLP suite or a BERT-based dependency parser) to analyze sentences from a source opinion corpus (e.g., Yelp reviews). We will then design a set of syntax-aware transformation rules (e.g., aspect-term substitution, modifier replacement, conjunction swapping) that preserve grammaticality while altering domain-specific lexical items. These transformations, guided by the parse trees, will generate novel, domain-pertinent training examples. The augmented dataset will be used to fine-tune a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., BART or PEGASUS) for the target summarization task, with performance evaluated against standard few-shot and data-scarce baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, linguistically grounded framework for data augmentation in text generation, specifically for opinion summarization. It will provide a methodology for rapidly bootstrapping summarization models in new domains with minimal human effort. The resulting models and the parser-based augmentation toolkit will be released as a resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation via back-translation or simple word substitution is common, the use of a syntactic parser to enforce grammatical correctness and enable complex, aspect-aware transformations for opinionated text generation is novel. This approach directly connects structured linguistic analysis with data scarcity solutions in generation, a link less explored in pre-2026 summarization work.

proposals.title: Logic-to-Text Generation with Uncertainty-Aware Decomposition and Contextual Merging
proposals.motivation: While semantic parsing (language to logic) is well-studied, the inverse task of generating fluent, faithful natural language from logical forms is crucial for explainable AI and human-computer interaction. Current neural approaches often produce generic or unfaithful text, lacking mechanisms to handle the compositional complexity of logic and to quantify generation confidence, limiting their reliability in critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A model that decomposes a logical form into verified, aspect-aware sub-structures and then hierarchically merges their surface realizations with explicit uncertainty quantification will generate more faithful, fluent, and contextually appropriate descriptions than end-to-end generation.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage encoder-decoder architecture. First, a decomposition module, inspired by verified aspect-aware summarization, breaks the input logical form (e.g., AMR, DRS) into coherent sub-graphs. Second, a context-aware hierarchical merging module, adapted from long-document summarization, sequentially generates and refines text spans for each sub-graph, building a coherent whole. Crucially, we integrate uncertainty quantification techniques from Retrieval-Augmented QA to produce confidence scores for each generated segment, enabling selective regeneration or human-in-the-loop verification.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, controllable framework for faithful logic-to-text generation, advancing interpretability. It will contribute new evaluation metrics incorporating uncertainty and faithfulness, and release a benchmark dataset pairing diverse logical forms with multiple high-quality textual descriptions.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of decomposition, verified generation, and explicit uncertainty quantification for logic-to-text is unexplored. It uniquely combines Mirella Lapata's expertise in structured generation, summarization merging, and uncertainty-aware QA into a new, principled solution for this inverse problem.

proposals.title: Differentiable Span Selection for Verifiably Grounded Abstractive Generation
proposals.motivation: Abstractive generation models, while fluent, often produce outputs that are not faithfully grounded in the source text, leading to hallucinations and a lack of verifiability. This is a critical issue for high-stakes applications like question answering and summarization, where trustworthiness is paramount. Existing methods for improving faithfulness, such as posterior regularization or constrained decoding, often lack end-to-end differentiability or provide only indirect control over grounding.
proposals.hypothesis: By introducing an explicit, trainable span selection module that produces a sparse, differentiable attention mask over the source, we can enforce a direct and verifiable link between the source text and the generated output, leading to improved factual consistency without sacrificing fluency.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder first processes the source document. A novel span selection module, implemented via a Gumbel-Softmax or Sparsemax layer over token-level scores, produces a sparse, differentiable mask highlighting salient spans. This mask gates the encoder's representations via element-wise multiplication before they are passed to the decoder for generation. The model is trained with a multi-task loss: a supervised extractive loss (e.g., binary cross-entropy) against ground-truth evidence spans (when available) and a standard conditional language modeling loss for the final generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, transparent framework for controllable text generation where the model's grounding decisions are explicit and verifiable. It will advance methods for building more trustworthy NLP systems by directly bridging extractive and abstractive paradigms. The proposed differentiable span selector is a general-purpose module applicable to summarization, QA, and data-to-text tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses latent or soft attention, our method enforces explicit, sparse span selection as a discrete-but-differentiable intermediate step, making grounding directly optimizable and inspectable. This integrates verifiable extractive reasoning into the core of the abstractive generation process in an end-to-end fashion.

proposals.title: Differentiable Extractive-Abstractive Fusion via Soft Latent Selection for Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current abstractive summarization models often generate unfaithful or ungrounded content, while extractive models lack flexibility. The dominant paradigm treats extraction and abstraction as separate, discrete stages, creating a pipeline that is not end-to-end trainable and can propagate errors. A unified, differentiable framework is needed to leverage the grounding of extraction within the fluency of abstraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the extractive selection of source content as a latent, continuous attention distribution—a soft, differentiable operator—and using it to directly modulate the context for a neural generator will yield more faithful and coherent summaries than pipelined or purely abstractive approaches.
proposals.method: We will design a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model where the encoder produces token-level representations. A novel 'Selection Gate' module will learn a continuous attention distribution over these tokens, acting as a soft extractive mask. This distribution will multiplicatively gate the encoder's feature maps before they are passed to the decoder's cross-attention, integrating selection into the generation process. The entire model, including the Selection Gate, will be trained end-to-end using a combined loss of maximum likelihood generation and a sparsity regularizer on the attention distribution to encourage focused selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end differentiable architecture that unifies extractive selection and abstractive generation. It will provide a new perspective on faithfulness in generation by making the model's grounding in the source text an explicit, learnable, and inspectable component. We will validate the approach on benchmark summarization datasets, measuring both performance and factual consistency.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual inversion of extraction from a discrete, pre-processing step to a latent, soft, and fully differentiable operator within the generation process, inspired by models of visual attention. This contrasts with existing hybrid models that typically perform hard extraction or use two-stage pipelines.

proposals.title: Adversarial Metric Robust Training for Human-Aligned Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Standard text generation models are trained to optimize automatic metrics like ROUGE, which are known to have significant biases, favoring surface-level lexical overlap over semantic fidelity, coherence, and other human-valued qualities. This metric bias leads to models that generate text that scores highly but is often judged as inferior by humans, creating a misalignment between automated evaluation and real-world utility. Addressing this misalignment is crucial for deploying reliable generation systems in summarization, dialogue, and other applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adversarial training framework, which pits a generator against a critic trained to distinguish human-preferred text from metric-optimized text, will force the generator to learn representations that capture human-valued qualities beyond lexical overlap, thereby producing text that is more robust to the known biases of standard evaluation metrics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the adversarial robustness framework from vision to text generation. A generator model (e.g., a fine-tuned T5 or BART) will produce candidate text. A critic model (a transformer-based discriminator) will be trained on human preference data (e.g., from summarization or dialogue evaluations) to distinguish human-written or human-preferred outputs from generator outputs optimized purely for ROUGE. The generator will then be trained with a dual objective: maximizing ROUGE (or similar metric) via reinforcement learning (e.g., with a PPO policy) while also minimizing the critic's ability to identify its output as metric-optimized, using a gradient reversal layer or a GAN-style adversarial loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm that explicitly mitigates metric bias, leading to text generation models whose outputs are better aligned with human judgment. It will provide a new, evaluation-aware adversarial robustness framework for NLP, moving beyond input perturbations to address objective function vulnerabilities. The resulting models and the critic's learned representations will offer insights into the gaps between automatic metrics and human preferences.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is common in NLP for data augmentation or style transfer, applying it specifically to create metric-robust generators by adversarially training against a human-preference critic is a novel conceptual and technical adaptation of robustness principles from computer vision. This directly targets the objective function bias, rather than input noise, which is a less explored direction in pre-2026 robustness research.

proposals.title: Adaptive-Compression Summarization: Dynamic Length Control via Information-Density Sampling
proposals.motivation: Current abstractive summarization systems predominantly generate summaries of a fixed, pre-specified length, a constraint that fails to reflect the inherent variability in document information density and redundancy. This one-size-fits-all approach can lead to over-compression of complex content or verbose, unfocused summaries for simple documents, degrading both faithfulness and utility. A principled, content-aware method for determining summary length on-the-fly is a critical open problem for producing high-quality, efficient summaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling a document as a non-stationary information signal and applying adaptive sampling principles from signal processing will enable a summarizer to dynamically allocate summary length proportional to local information density, thereby generating more faithful and concise summaries than fixed-length baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage encoder-decoder architecture. First, a density estimator module, built upon a pre-trained language model (e.g., BART or T5), will analyze the source document to produce a per-segment information density score, integrating local novelty (via self-attention) and global salience (via a document-level graph). Second, a summarization decoder will use these scores as a soft, adaptive budget within a constrained attention mechanism, akin to variable bitrate allocation. A learned 'sufficiency' classifier, trained on summary-document pairs with human judgments of factual coverage, will determine the stopping point for generation, replacing the fixed token limit.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for content-adaptive summarization length, 2) a new task and evaluation protocol for dynamic-length summary quality beyond ROUGE, focusing on compression fidelity, and 3) a publicly released model that outperforms fixed-length and extractive-oracle baselines on metrics of factual consistency and information density alignment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal analogy to adaptive signal sampling and the integration of a trainable sufficiency model to control generation length, moving beyond simple heuristics or reinforcement learning with a fixed-length reward. This directly challenges the entrenched fixed-length assumption in neural summarization.

proposals.title: Strategic Sketching: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Iterative Sketch Refinement in Multi-Document Summarization
proposals.motivation: Multi-document summarization (MDS) often relies on extractive or abstractive methods that directly generate summaries, struggling with information integration and hallucination across many documents. While content sketching is a promising intermediate step, existing approaches treat it as a static, correctness-focused pre-processing task, ignoring its strategic role in the overall summarization quality. A dynamic, learnable sketching strategy could better balance the cost of sketch generation with the downstream benefits of improved coherence and reduced error.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative sketch refinement process, guided by a reinforcement learning agent that learns to rank and modify sketch elements based on long-term summary quality, will produce higher-quality multi-document summaries than static, correctness-first sketching approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical RL agent where the action space comprises operations to add, remove, or re-weight content units (e.g., sentences or propositions) in a dynamic sketch. The state is the current sketch and encoded source documents. The reward function will combine ROUGE/LCS against references with a learned 'hallucination penalty' model (inspired by pre-2026 QA verification work) and a cost for sketch complexity. Training will use a policy gradient method (e.g., PPO) with a transformer-based policy network, where the final summary is generated by a fine-tuned pre-trained model (e.g., BART or PEGASUS) conditioned on the refined sketch.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for treating sketch generation as a core, optimizable component of MDS. It will yield new insights into the cost-quality trade-off in intermediate representation design and deliver a practical system that reduces hallucinations through iterative, verified refinement.
proposals.novelty: It fundamentally re-conceptualizes the sketch from a static, minimal outline to a dynamic, strategically refined object, using RL to optimize its long-term utility rather than its immediate correctness—a departure from prior extractive or one-shot abstractive sketching methods.

proposals.title: Adversarially Regularized Reward Models for Robust and Faithful Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is central to aligning large language models, but reward models are vulnerable to reward hacking, where policies exploit superficial correlates of quality, leading to plausible but unfaithful or degenerate text. This problem is acute in complex generation tasks like summarization and question answering, where multi-dimensional preferences (e.g., faithfulness, coherence, coverage) must be balanced. Current methods, including direct preference optimization, lack explicit mechanisms to probe and harden reward models against such adversarial policy behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating active adversarial regularization during policy optimization will force the reward model to learn more robust representations of human preferences, leading to text generation policies that are more faithful and less prone to reward hacking.
proposals.method: We will first train a dense, multi-dimensional reward model on human preference data for a target task (e.g., summarization). During policy fine-tuning via PPO, we will deploy a battery of adversarial probes—small generators trained to produce text that maximizes the primary reward but exhibits known failure modes (e.g., factual inconsistency, repetition). The policy's objective will be augmented with a regularization term that minimizes its reward on these adversarial examples, encouraging it to avoid the associated weaknesses. This creates a minimax game between the adversarial probes and the policy, both constrained by the same reward model.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel training framework for robust RLHF that explicitly mitigates reward hacking. It will provide new insights into the failure modes of reward models and contribute a diagnostic toolkit of adversarial probes. The resulting policies are expected to demonstrate improved faithfulness and overall quality in complex text generation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is known in robustness literature, its application as an *intrinsic regularizer within RLHF policy optimization* is novel. This approach directly connects reward model probing to policy learning, moving beyond post-hoc analysis or static dataset augmentation.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Reward Shaping for Text Generation via Neurosyntactic Integration
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for text generation often relies on a single, scalar reward model trained on preference data, which can be costly to obtain and may not capture diverse linguistic desiderata. This leads to models that optimize for a narrow definition of quality, potentially neglecting aspects like factual consistency, syntactic diversity, or semantic coherence. A principled, multi-faceted reward signal is needed to guide generation towards more robust and human-like text.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite reward function, shaped by a weighted blend of automated metrics (e.g., BERTScore for semantics, syntactic tree depth for structure, and entailment scores for factuality) and intermittent human preference feedback, will outperform single-objective RLHF baselines by producing text that is superior across a broader, more balanced set of quality dimensions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the multi-objective reward shaping framework from computational neuroscience, treating each automated metric as an intrinsic reward signal. For a target task like opinion summarization, we will define a reward R(s,a) = Σ_i w_i * r_i(s,a) + λ * r_h(s,a), where r_i are normalized metric-based rewards and r_h is a sparse human feedback signal. A policy (e.g., a T5 or BART model) will be fine-tuned via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). We will experiment with dynamic weighting schemes (w_i) inspired by dopaminergic balancing and employ techniques from our prior work on uncertainty quantification to assess reward reliability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable framework for designing composite rewards for text generation, moving beyond monolithic reward models. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art model for a challenging generation task (e.g., decomposed opinion summarization) and a systematic analysis of how different reward components influence various textual properties.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct conceptual and technical transfer of multi-objective reward balancing models from computational neuroscience to NLP's RLHF paradigm, creating a structured, hybrid reward from disparate automated metrics and human feedback. This differs from prior work that typically either uses a single learned reward or simple linear combinations of a few scores like ROUGE.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Remedying RL-Based Ranker Generalization Failures: A Decomposable, Aspect-Aware Framework
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for ranking has shown strong in-domain performance, but its failure modes when deployed to new domains or languages are poorly understood. This lack of diagnostic tools hinders the development of robust, generalizable ranking systems, as failures are often attributed holistically rather than isolated to specific components like the reward model or policy. Building on recent work in uncertainty quantification and modular verification for NLP tasks, a systematic framework is needed to decompose and remedy these failures.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalization failures of RL ranking systems can be isolated to specific, measurable axes of domain shift (e.g., query-document relationship divergence, reward sparsity) and linked to particular system components, enabling the design of targeted, effective mitigation strategies.
proposals.method: We will construct a suite of diagnostic testbeds by systematically varying key axes (e.g., linguistic features via controlled perturbations, user behavior patterns via synthetic logs, and query-document relationship complexity). Using a hierarchical, modular RL ranking architecture, we will run controlled ablation studies to attribute performance drops to components like the reward model or policy network. Mitigations will be tested, including context-aware reward shaping (inspired by hierarchical merging) and deploying verified, aspect-aware modules for handling specific failure modes, similar to techniques in decomposed summarization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a publicly available diagnostic framework for stress-testing RL rankers, 2) a taxonomy of failure modes linked to system components and domain shifts, and 3) a set of validated, targeted mitigation strategies. This moves the field from observing cross-domain performance drops to fundamentally understanding and fixing them.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior cross-domain evaluations that report aggregate metrics, this work proposes a controlled, decomposable diagnostic approach that isolates failure causality. It uniquely integrates concepts from uncertainty quantification and verified modular design into the RL ranking paradigm for robust generalization.

proposals.title: Mapping the Adaptive Niche of RL Rankers: A Cross-Domain and Cross-Lingual Stress Test via Ecological Survey Methods
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for ranking has shown strong performance in specific domains like news summarization, but its generalizability remains poorly understood. As NLP systems are deployed in diverse real-world contexts—from legal documents to multilingual social media—it is critical to systematically evaluate their robustness and failure modes across this heterogeneous landscape. This work addresses the pressing need for rigorous, standardized evaluation beyond narrow benchmarks to ensure reliable performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a standardized evaluation 'transect' across diverse domain-language 'biomes' will reveal a non-uniform performance landscape, identifying specific combinations (e.g., low-resource language in a technical domain) that act as critical stressors, thereby delineating the fundamental boundaries of an RL ranker's transferable capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RL ranking framework from recent QA and summarization work (e.g., RLHF for answer ranking). The core method involves constructing a structured evaluation suite comprising non-news datasets from domains like legal (e.g., EUR-Lex), scientific (e.g., arXiv), and social media (e.g., Reddit), paired with multiple languages (e.g., English, Spanish, a lower-resource language). We will apply a fixed battery of metrics—including standard performance (ROUGE, BLEU, accuracy), robustness (e.g., consistency under perturbation), and uncertainty measures (inspired by UQ in QA)—uniformly across all combinations. Analysis will involve clustering performance drops and correlating them with dataset characteristics (e.g., lexical diversity, structural complexity).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic 'map' of RL ranking generalizability, pinpointing precise domain-language interactions that degrade performance. It will contribute a novel, reusable evaluation methodology inspired by ecological survey design to the NLP community, moving beyond single-domain benchmarks. The findings will offer concrete guidance for model development and deployment in cross-domain and multilingual settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct analogy to ecological survey methods to structure evaluation, creating a standardized 'transect' for stress testing. While multi-domain evaluation exists, this systematic, simultaneous variation of domain and language to map a model's 'adaptive niche' is a non-obvious and rigorous approach to understanding generalizability boundaries.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain and Cross-Lingual Generalization of RL-Based Ranking for Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning (RL) ranking methods have shown strong performance in text generation tasks like summarization and QA, but their evaluation is often limited to English news domains (e.g., CNN/DM). This creates a significant gap in understanding their robustness and generalizability. For real-world deployment, models must perform reliably across diverse domains (e.g., scientific literature, legal documents, customer reviews) and languages, where linguistic structure, discourse patterns, and information density vary substantially.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RL ranking approaches, while effective on news, will exhibit performance degradation on out-of-domain and non-English data due to domain-specific stylistic conventions and language-specific structural dependencies, but that this degradation can be mitigated through targeted, lightweight adaptation strategies.
proposals.method: We will take a state-of-the-art RL ranking model (e.g., a policy gradient method like REINFORCE or a reward-augmented maximum likelihood approach) pre-trained on news summarization/QA. We will conduct a systematic evaluation on curated datasets spanning domains (e.g., arXiv for science, EU legislation for law, Amazon reviews) and languages (e.g., German, Chinese, Arabic) using pre-2026 multilingual models like mT5 or mBART. We will then analyze failure modes and implement adaptation via (1) domain/language-aware reward shaping within the RL framework and (2) few-shot prompting of the underlying generator, measuring the performance-cost trade-off.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of RL ranking generalization for generation, identifying key failure points across domains and languages. It will contribute a practical analysis framework and adaptation techniques to improve model robustness, moving beyond single-domain, English-centric evaluation. The findings will inform best practices for deploying RL-tuned text generation models in multilingual and multi-domain settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Lapata and others has focused on uncertainty, context-aware merging, and aspect verification within single domains/languages, systematically evaluating and adapting the core RL ranking paradigm itself for cross-domain and cross-lingual generalization is an unexplored direction critical for the technology's broader applicability.

proposals.title: Diversified Submodular Selection via Portfolio-Theoretic Dispersion: A Framework for Summarization and Retrieval
proposals.motivation: In NLP tasks like summarization and retrieval-augmented QA, selecting a non-redundant, high-quality subset from a large candidate set is crucial for performance and user trust. Pre-2026, submodular optimization is a standard tool for set selection, but its diversity-promoting variants often rely on heuristic coverage functions or separate repulsion terms that lack a principled, tunable balance between quality and diversity. This can lead to suboptimal information coverage and redundancy, impacting downstream reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling pairwise item similarity within a submodular objective, inspired by Modern Portfolio Theory's risk-diversification framework, will yield more controllable and effective diverse selection for NLP tasks than existing heuristic approaches.
proposals.method: We will design a new submodular function F(S) = λ * L(S) - (1-λ) * Σ_{i,j in S} K_ij, where L(S) is a monotone submodular coverage term (e.g., facility location, log-det), K is a precomputed similarity kernel between items, and λ controls the trade-off. This directly penalizes selection of similar items. We will develop efficient greedy algorithms with theoretical guarantees for this non-monotone function. The framework will be evaluated on context selection for multi-document summarization (using datasets from the researcher's prior work) and document retrieval for QA, benchmarking against methods like Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs) and other diversity-aware submodular functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled optimization framework for diverse selection in NLP, grounded in financial theory. It will provide a tunable mechanism to explicitly manage the coverage-diversity trade-off, improving the quality and non-redundancy of selected subsets in summarization and retrieval contexts. The resulting algorithms and analysis will extend the toolkit for structured prediction and subset selection.
proposals.novelty: While DPPs also model diversity via determinants, our portfolio-inspired dispersion term integrated into a weighted submodular objective offers a different, more flexible, and interpretable formulation for controlling redundancy, directly adapting a proven financial optimization principle to NLP set selection problems.

proposals.title: Response-Adaptive Exploration for Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning in Text Summarization
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is increasingly used to optimize summarization models for non-differentiable metrics like ROUGE or human preferences. However, standard RL exploration strategies, such as epsilon-greedy or contextual bandits, are often undirected and sample-inefficient, leading to high-variance policy updates and slow convergence. This inefficiency is particularly acute in the complex, structured action space of summarization, where actions correspond to selecting, compressing, or paraphrasing text spans.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the principles of response-adaptive randomization from clinical trial design to structure RL exploration will yield a more sample-efficient and stable policy learning process for summarization, outperforming standard exploration baselines in terms of convergence speed and final performance.
proposals.method: We will frame the RL summarization agent's action space (e.g., sentence selection or compression operations) as 'treatment arms'. Using a Thompson Sampling or Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) bandit framework, we will maintain and update a posterior distribution over the expected reward (e.g., ROUGE-L or BERTScore) for each action type conditioned on the input context. The exploration policy will then dynamically bias action selection proportionally to these interim reward estimates, ensuring more sampling of promising actions while guaranteeing a minimum probability for all actions to maintain exploration. This adaptive policy will be integrated into a policy gradient (e.g., PPO) or a REINFORCE-based training loop for an encoder-decoder summarization model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled exploration strategy for RL in NLP that directly addresses sample inefficiency. It will provide a framework for incorporating structured, uncertainty-aware exploration into summarization, potentially leading to models that learn higher-quality policies with fewer training episodes. The research will also demonstrate a successful cross-disciplinary analogy between clinical trial methodology and machine learning optimization.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive randomization is well-established in biostatistics, its application to structure exploration in RL for text generation is novel. This approach moves beyond treating exploration as a generic, context-agnostic process, instead creating a guided, variance-reduced strategy informed by interim performance feedback, which is non-obvious given the standard use of simple heuristics or pure bandit methods in prior NLP RL work.

proposals.title: Zero-Shot Summarization via Reinforcement Learning with Decomposed Reward Shaping
proposals.motivation: Current abstractive summarization models require large, domain-specific datasets, limiting their application to specialized or low-resource domains where such data is unavailable. Pre-2026, while large language models (LLMs) offer zero-shot capabilities, their outputs for summarization are often generic and lack controlled, task-specific coherence. There is a critical need for frameworks that can adapt high-level summarization objectives to new domains without fine-tuning on in-domain examples.
proposals.hypothesis: A reinforcement learning (RL) framework, guided by a decomposed set of generalizable, aspect-aware reward functions derived from model confidence and input context, can enable effective zero-shot abstractive summarization that matches the performance of few-shot fine-tuned models.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RL fine-tuning paradigm for summarization to a zero-shot setting. The core is a reward model decomposed into verified, aspect-aware modules (inspired by Lapata's prior work) for coherence, relevance, and factual consistency, computed using pre-trained models (e.g., question answering for verification, NLI for entailment). We will integrate uncertainty quantification from retrieval-augmented QA to weight rewards based on the LLM's confidence in its generations. The policy, a large pre-trained language model (e.g., T5, GPT-3), will be optimized via PPO against these frozen, general-purpose rewards without any domain-specific summarization training pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for zero-shot summarization that moves beyond simple prompting, offering a robust, tunable framework for domain adaptation. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating zero-shot summarization in specialized domains and advance understanding of how decomposed, verifiable rewards can guide LLM generation in the absence of supervision.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of a decomposed, aspect-aware reward architecture—typically used for supervised opinion summarization—with an RL framework for zero-shot learning, crucially augmented by uncertainty-aware reward weighting to handle the lack of ground-truth data.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Multi-Granular Merging for Extreme-Length Document Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current long document summarization models, often tested on articles or reports, struggle with the extreme length and complex, multi-level discourse structure of books, legal codes, or lengthy scientific articles. This limitation hinders the application of NLP to critical domains like legal analysis, literature review, and scientific synthesis, where comprehensive understanding of documents spanning hundreds of pages is required. Existing methods lack efficient mechanisms to manage the overwhelming context and preserve crucial information across vast textual distances.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, multi-granular merging architecture, guided by learned discourse-aware salience, can effectively scale summarization to book-length documents by recursively integrating fine-grained local information into coherent, high-level summaries while mitigating catastrophic forgetting and information loss.
proposals.method: We will extend the Context-Aware Hierarchical Merging framework to operate over multiple granularities (e.g., paragraph, section, chapter). The model will employ a two-stage process: first, a decomposed, aspect-aware module (inspired by Decomposed Opinion Summarization) will extract verified factual or thematic units from small segments. Second, a novel multi-tier merging network will hierarchically fuse these units, using a learned gating mechanism informed by a discourse parser and uncertainty quantification (from Uncertainty Quantification in Retrieval Augmented QA) to weight the importance and reliability of information from different document depths. Training will leverage a curriculum learning strategy on constructed datasets of books and legal documents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable architecture for extreme-length summarization, a task largely unexplored in pre-2026 research. It will contribute a new benchmark dataset for book and legal document summarization and advance the understanding of how to model and preserve discourse structure and long-range dependencies in generative NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of discourse structure to guide the hierarchical merging process across multiple granularities, combined with uncertainty-aware gating to manage information flow from vastly longer contexts than previously attempted in end-to-end summarization systems.

proposals.title: Inverse Expansion: Iterative Context Selection for Coherent Document Generation via Tomographic Regularization
proposals.motivation: Current document expansion and long-form generation models often produce text that is factually inconsistent or narratively incoherent, as they typically perform one-shot retrieval and generation. This is a critical bottleneck for applications like detailed report writing from brief summaries or creating comprehensive documents from outlines. A principled, iterative method that dynamically refines source context based on generation quality is needed to ensure structural integrity.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing expansion as an inverse problem—iteratively selecting context to best explain a target summary—and regularizing the process with seismic tomography-inspired constraints for coherence, we can generate longer documents that are more factually consistent and narratively coherent than those from one-shot expansion methods.
proposals.method: We will develop an iterative algorithm where a retriever (e.g., DPR or a dense encoder) selects context chunks. A generator (a fine-tuned T5 or GPT-2/Neo model) produces a document section. A discrepancy module, using sentence embeddings (e.g., SBERT) and entailment classifiers, compares the generated text's semantic 'waveform' to the original summary. This error signal, combined with a narrative coherence regularizer (modeled on tomography's smoothness constraints via graph-based attention over the generated text), guides the next retrieval step. The process repeats until a reconstruction loss converges.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel iterative framework for controllable document expansion that explicitly optimizes for narrative coherence. It will yield a new benchmark and evaluation metrics for long-form generation consistency, moving beyond ROUGE and BLEU. The method will advance techniques for merging and verifying information across long contexts, a core challenge in summarization and QA.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct analogy to seismic tomography's inverse problem solution, translating iterative forward modeling and regularization into a NLP context selection loop. This moves beyond static retrieval-augmented generation to a dynamic, self-correcting expansion process where generation quality directly informs subsequent retrieval.

proposals.title: Iterative, Feedback-Driven Context Refinement for Robust Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Current retrieval-augmented QA systems often treat context retrieval as a static, one-off preprocessing step, which can lead to incomplete or biased context selection. This is particularly problematic for complex questions requiring multi-step reasoning or verification across a long document. A dynamic, question-guided refinement process is needed to improve robustness and accuracy, directly addressing the uncertainty quantification challenges highlighted in recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative, closed-loop system that uses the QA model's internal attention and confidence signals to guide progressive context refinement will yield more accurate and verifiable answers than static retrieval methods, especially for questions requiring nuanced reasoning.
proposals.method: We propose a two-module system: a lightweight, trainable sentence relevance scorer (e.g., based on BERT) and a core QA model (e.g., T5 or FiD). The process begins with the scorer fetching a broad initial context. The QA model processes it, and its token-level attention distributions or answer confidence scores are used to formulate a feedback signal. This signal, encoding what the model found supportive or contradictory, guides the relevance scorer to re-weight or retrieve additional sentences from the full document in a second pass. This loop continues for a fixed number of iterations or until answer confidence stabilizes, implementing a form of decomposed, aspect-aware verification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for tightly integrating retrieval and reasoning, moving beyond pipeline architectures. It will yield a new method for dynamic, question-sensitive context selection that improves answer fidelity and offers intrinsic explainability through the refinement trace.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, iterative feedback loop where the reasoning model directly guides retrieval updates, contrasting with standard retrieve-then-answer pipelines or single-pass re-ranking methods. This turns context selection into an active, hypothesis-driven process.

proposals.title: Semantic Filtering for Scalable Question Answering: A Denoising Preprocessing Framework
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented QA systems struggle with long-context inputs, as irrelevant information acts as noise, degrading performance and increasing computational cost. Current methods often process entire retrieved passages, forcing the QA model to reason over redundant or distracting text. A principled, learnable preprocessing step to filter this semantic noise is needed to scale QA to longer documents efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: A sentence importance ranking model, trained as a semantic filter, can denoise long-context inputs for QA systems, leading to improved answer accuracy and significant reductions in inference time and memory usage compared to processing full contexts.
proposals.method: We will adapt the hierarchical merging and aspect-aware verification techniques from Lapata's summarization work to build a context-aware sentence ranker. This ranker, using a lightweight transformer encoder, will score sentences for relevance to the query and internal coherence. We will integrate it as a preprocessing module before a frozen, pre-trained QA model (e.g., T5 or FiD) on datasets like HotpotQA and QASPER. The system will be trained end-to-end, with the filter's gradients informed by the final QA loss, ensuring it learns to retain crucial evidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, efficient architecture for long-context QA that explicitly decouples noise filtering from answer generation. It will yield a new benchmark for the efficiency-accuracy trade-off in QA and a reusable, interpretable semantic filtering module applicable to other NLP tasks requiring long-document reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovatively frames sentence ranking as an analogical 'denoising filter' from signal processing, creating a critical preprocessing stage rather than an auxiliary task. It uniquely leverages summarization-based verification for filter training, ensuring the filtered context retains factual completeness for QA.

proposals.title: Selective Context for Robust QA: A Dual-Task Framework for Sentence Importance Ranking and Answer Verification
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented QA systems often struggle with long, noisy contexts, where irrelevant sentences can distract the reader model and degrade answer accuracy. Pre-2026 methods for handling long documents in summarization, such as hierarchical merging, demonstrate the value of identifying salient content, but this principle is not systematically applied to improve QA robustness. A dedicated sentence importance model, acting as a selective filter, could enhance QA performance and provide interpretable uncertainty signals by highlighting the evidence used for answering.
proposals.hypothesis: A sentence ranking model, trained jointly with a QA verification objective, can effectively pre-filter input contexts to improve the accuracy and robustness of downstream QA systems, particularly on long-document or multi-document inputs.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, end-to-end trainable architecture. First, a transformer-based sentence ranker, inspired by extractive summarization models, processes the retrieved context to assign importance scores, potentially using a hierarchical encoder for long documents. Second, a filtered subset of top-ranked sentences is passed to a standard QA reader (e.g., a span predictor). Crucially, we introduce a joint training objective combining a ranking loss (e.g., based on ROUGE or QA-supervision from sentence-level answerability) with the standard QA loss. We will evaluate using datasets like Natural Questions or HotpotQA, comparing against baselines that use full or randomly filtered context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable preprocessing module for QA that reduces computational overhead and noise. It will provide a new framework for quantifying uncertainty in QA by linking answer confidence to the quality and clarity of the ranked evidential sentences. The resulting model advances methods for context-aware reasoning in information-intensive NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: While sentence ranking is established in summarization, its application as a trainable, integrated pre-filter for QA, coupled with a joint verification objective, is novel. This moves beyond simple heuristic retrieval or post-hoc explanation to make importance ranking a core, optimizing component of the QA pipeline.

proposals.title: Stencil-Optimized Primitives for Real-Time, Edge-Device Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Deploying high-performance question answering (QA) models on resource-constrained edge devices is critical for real-time applications, from personal assistants to industrial diagnostics. Current approaches rely on generic model compression and graph compilers, which often fail to achieve the extreme latency and throughput required due to suboptimal mapping of core operations to specific hardware. Drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics, where stencil optimization makes complex simulations tractable, we propose a fundamental shift: building models from hand-optimized, hardware-native computational kernels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by identifying and deeply optimizing the fundamental, repeated computational 'stencils' (e.g., specific attention patterns, feed-forward layer contractions) of a QA model for a target System-on-Chip (SoC), we can construct a model that significantly outperforms those generated by generic compilers in latency and throughput, without sacrificing answer quality.
proposals.method: We will first profile a state-of-the-art, compact QA model (e.g., a distilled Transformer) to identify its most frequent and costly tensor operation patterns. For each identified 'stencil,' we will apply hardware-specific optimizations—including loop tiling, unrolling, and memory layout transformations—tailored for a target mobile SoC (e.g., a specific ARM CPU with NPU). These optimized primitives will be implemented in low-level code (e.g., C++ with SIMD intrinsics, custom NPU instructions). Finally, we will rebuild the model's computational graph using these primitives and evaluate it on edge-device QA benchmarks, comparing against frameworks like TensorFlow Lite and TVM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodology for constructing edge ML models from hardware-optimized primitives, 2) a set of open-source, optimized kernel libraries for key QA operations on common mobile SoCs, and 3) an empirical demonstration of the latency-throughput gains achievable over compiler-driven approaches for a critical NLP task.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the CFD concept of stencil optimization to neural network inference, moving beyond graph-level compilation to focus on the manual, hardware-aware optimization of fundamental computational patterns—a level of specialization not pursued in mainstream ML deployment tools pre-2026.

proposals.title: Latent-Selective Generation: A Single-Pass Architecture for Real-Time Abstractive Summarization on Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of mobile and edge computing demands NLP models that are both high-quality and resource-efficient. Current abstractive summarization models are too computationally heavy for real-time use on edge devices, while faster extractive models produce less fluent, context-poor summaries. This creates a critical gap for applications like real-time news digests, meeting note generation, and accessibility tools, where low latency and coherent abstraction are essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, end-to-end neural model with a lightweight, latent selection mechanism can perform soft, attention-based content selection and constrained abstraction in one forward pass, achieving the fluency of abstractive models with the latency profile of extractive ones, suitable for edge deployment.
proposals.method: We will develop a transformer-based encoder-decoder where the encoder uses a novel, sparsity-constrained attention head to produce a latent selection distribution over input tokens. This distribution directly gates the cross-attention in a heavily pruned decoder, forcing it to focus on salient content. The entire model will be trained with a combined objective of selection fidelity (via a differentiable proxy for ROUGE-based extraction) and abstractive fluency, followed by post-training quantization and dynamic module pruning for edge optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, fusion-based architecture that eliminates the pipeline overhead of traditional two-stage summarizers, 2) a methodology for training latent selection jointly with generation, and 3) an empirical benchmark for abstractive summarization performance under strict, measurable edge-computing constraints.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in inverting the standard pipeline by performing implicit, soft selection within a single-generation pass, moving beyond simply compressing existing large models or hybrid extractive-abstractive systems that require multiple computational steps.

proposals.title: End-to-End Document Processing with Adaptive Span Transformers
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for tasks like question answering and summarization typically rely on pre-segmented sentences as input, introducing a pipeline dependency on potentially noisy, domain-specific sentence tokenizers. This segmentation can disrupt discourse coherence, misrepresent long-range dependencies, and degrade performance on complex document-level reasoning. A model that processes raw document text directly would be more robust and better reflect the true structure of language.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer-based architecture that learns to dynamically segment and attend to meaningful text spans within raw documents will outperform sentence-segmented baselines on tasks requiring cross-sentence reasoning and long-context understanding.
proposals.method: We will develop a variant of the Transformer encoder (e.g., Longformer or BigBird) that operates on raw character or subword sequences of entire documents. We will integrate a lightweight, trainable boundary detection module, inspired by boundary-aware objectives in segmentation tasks, to induce latent segment representations. The model will be trained end-to-end on a multi-task objective combining a primary task (e.g., question answering from Lapata's prior work) with auxiliary losses that encourage coherent span formation, evaluated on datasets like HotpotQA or PubMed for long-document summarization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework for document-level NLP that removes the sentence segmentation bottleneck, improving robustness and portability across domains. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating models on raw text and offer insights into how neural architectures can learn implicit discourse structures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on long-context transformers or hierarchical models over fixed segments, our approach uniquely integrates adaptive, latent segmentation directly into the end-to-end learning process, moving beyond the assumption that sentences are the optimal unit of processing for all tasks.

proposals.title: Modeling Explicit Discourse Dependencies for Coherent Extractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Extractive summarization models, even those based on Transformers, often treat sentence selection as independent classification, implicitly relying on stacked self-attention layers to capture selection dependencies. This can lead to incoherent summaries with missing discourse links or redundant information, as the model lacks an explicit, structured mechanism to enforce narrative flow or argumentative consistency between selected sentences. This problem is acute in long-document and multi-document summarization, where global coherence is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling inter-sentence dependencies—such as discourse relations, coreference chains, and topical progression—within the extractive selection process will yield more coherent and informative summaries than models relying solely on implicit representations from stacked Transformer layers.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage neural architecture. First, a base Transformer encoder (e.g., BERT or Longformer) processes the document. Second, we introduce a Graph Neural Network (GNN) layer operating over a sentence-level graph, where nodes are sentence representations and edges are weighted by pre-computed discourse relations (e.g., from a pre-2026 parser like DLP) and semantic similarity. This GNN explicitly propagates selection signals between dependent sentences. The final extractor will be a joint pointer network conditioned on both the base encoder and GNN outputs, trained end-to-end with a coherence-aware objective that penalizes breaks in discourse flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for injecting explicit discourse structure into neural extractive summarization, moving beyond implicit dependency modeling. We will release a new benchmark for evaluating summary coherence based on discourse metrics and demonstrate performance gains on long-document datasets (e.g., arXiv, GovReport).
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used in text, their integration here to explicitly enforce *extractive selection dependencies* defined by linguistic discourse graphs is novel, contrasting with prior work that uses graphs for representation enrichment alone or focuses on abstractive generation.

proposals.title: Efficient Two-Stage Summarization via Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning and Dynamic Curriculum Learning
proposals.motivation: Two-stage fine-tuning, which first trains a model on a large corpus of general text and then on a specific summarization dataset, is effective for abstractive summarization but computationally prohibitive. This cost limits accessibility and experimentation, especially for long-document summarization where models like BART and PEGASUS are large. Reducing this cost without sacrificing performance is a critical challenge for scalable NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically applying Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA or adapters in the second stage, and by employing a dynamic curriculum learning strategy in the first stage, we can drastically reduce computational cost while matching or exceeding the performance of full two-stage fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will first pre-train a base model (e.g., BART-large) on a general corpus using a dynamic curriculum that prioritizes easier, more general examples early, accelerating convergence. For the second, task-specific stage, we will replace full fine-tuning with a PEFT method, rigorously comparing LoRA, adapter layers, and prefix-tuning. We will evaluate efficiency (FLOPs, time) and performance (ROUGE, BERTScore, human evaluation) on benchmarks like CNN/DM and GovReport, contrasting our approach against standard two-stage fine-tuning and single-stage baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, efficient framework for two-stage summarization fine-tuning, making advanced summarization models more accessible. It will provide a systematic analysis of which PEFT methods are most effective for preserving knowledge during task adaptation in summarization, contributing new insights to the fields of efficient NLP and transfer learning.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT methods exist, their targeted application to optimize the distinct stages of summarization fine-tuning is unexplored. Combining this with a dynamic curriculum for the initial pre-training stage creates a novel, holistic approach to efficiency that goes beyond simply applying a single known technique.

proposals.title: Jointly Optimized Extraction and Abstraction for Coherent Long-Form Generation
proposals.motivation: Current approaches to tasks like long document summarization and retrieval-augmented question answering often separate extractive content selection from abstractive rewriting, leading to suboptimal coherence and potential information loss. A unified end-to-end model could better align these complementary objectives, improving the factual grounding and fluency of generated text, which is critical for high-stakes applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A single model jointly trained with extractive and abstractive objectives will outperform cascaded or purely abstractive systems by learning a more coherent and factually consistent representation for generation, particularly for long-context tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture with a two-tier decoding head. The encoder processes the source document(s). The first head performs token-level extractive selection via a learned gating mechanism, producing a soft content mask. The second, abstractive head conditions its generation on both the original encoder states and the gated content representation. The model is trained with a combined loss: a content selection loss (e.g., binary cross-entropy for salient spans) and a standard negative log-likelihood loss for the abstractive output. We will leverage pre-trained models like BART or T5 as a starting point and evaluate on benchmarks for summarization (e.g., Multi-News) and QA (e.g., HotpotQA).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, end-to-end framework for joint extractive-abstractive processing, advancing methodology in text generation. It will deliver empirical evidence on the benefits of joint optimization for output quality and factual consistency, directly contributing to Mirella Lapata's research themes on context-aware reasoning and verified information synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses pipeline models or multi-task learning with separate heads, our approach integrates extraction as a latent, differentiable conditioning signal within a single generative process, enabling deeper interaction between the two objectives during both training and inference.

proposals.title: Verifiable Copy Mechanisms for Factual Consistency in Abstractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Abstractive summarization models often generate fluent but factually inconsistent text, hallucinating details not present in the source. This is a critical failure mode for high-stakes applications like legal or medical document summarization. While pointer-generator networks can copy tokens to improve faithfulness, their operation is opaque and they lack mechanisms to verify the factual grounding of copied content.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a pointer-generator copy mechanism with a trainable, aspect-aware verification module will produce more factually consistent summaries by explicitly controlling and validating what information is copied from the source.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based abstractive summarizer (e.g., BART or PEGASUS) with a pointer-generator network to compute a dynamic generation probability for each decoder step, choosing between generating a vocabulary word or copying from the source. Crucially, we will integrate a verification module inspired by decomposed opinion summarization, which learns to project the decoder's hidden state and the pointer-attention distribution over source tokens onto predefined factual aspects (e.g., entities, quantities, actions). This module will produce a consistency score used to re-weight the copy distribution and provide an auxiliary training signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel architecture that enhances factual faithfulness in abstractive summarization through verified copying. It will provide a new framework for quantifying and controlling the uncertainty associated with generated versus copied content, advancing methods for trustworthy text generation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of the copy mechanism with an explicit, aspect-aware verification module, moving beyond simple pointer networks to create a self-verifying generation process. This directly addresses the uncertainty in retrieval-augmented generation by making the copy operation auditable and trainable for factual grounding.

proposals.title: Semantic Role-Enhanced Entity Grids for Coherent Multi-Document Summarization
proposals.motivation: The classic entity-grid model captures local coherence via syntactic patterns of entity realization (subject, object), but it ignores the rich semantic relations (e.g., Agent-Patient, Located-In) that define how entities interact. This limits its ability to model global discourse structure and assess the coherence of summaries that merge information from multiple documents, a core challenge in multi-document summarization and retrieval-augmented generation. A model incorporating semantic roles can better evaluate and generate summaries where factual consistency and relational flow are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting the entity-grid representation with predicate-argument structures and semantic role labels (e.g., from a pre-2026 SRL model like AllenNLP's) will provide a more accurate and robust signal for modeling document and summary coherence than syntactic patterns alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the entity-grid to a Semantic Relation Grid (SRG), where cells track not only an entity's grammatical function but also its semantic role (Agent, Patient, Location, etc.) relative to predicates in each sentence. Using a pre-trained semantic role labeler, we will construct SRGs for source documents and candidate summaries. A neural scoring model (e.g., a transformer encoder or graph neural network operating on the SRG) will be trained to predict summary quality metrics (e.g., coherence, factual consistency) on datasets like Multi-News or DUC. This model will be integrated into a summarization pipeline as a reranking or planning component.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable representation for document coherence that bridges syntax and semantics. It will yield a trainable model for evaluating and improving the coherence of multi-document summaries, directly advancing methods for context-aware merging and verified summarization. The resulting framework will offer insights into the relational structures that underpin coherent information fusion.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses entity grids for coherence, this proposal uniquely integrates formal semantic role labeling into the grid structure to model predicate-argument relations explicitly, moving beyond surface syntax. This combines discourse modeling with semantic parsing in a way not explored in existing entity-grid or recent neural coherence literature.

proposals.title: Graph-Based Uncertainty Propagation for Robust Multi-Hop Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented QA systems, especially for multi-hop questions, are brittle to incomplete or noisy knowledge graphs and retrieved passages. Pre-2026 methods for uncertainty quantification in QA often treat the reasoning chain as a sequence, failing to model the relational structure between entities and the propagation of uncertainty through this network, which is critical for identifying and rectifying reasoning failures.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the entity-relation network of a multi-hop QA problem as a probabilistic graph will allow for explicit quantification and propagation of uncertainty across reasoning paths, leading to more robust answer selection and the identification of critical knowledge gaps.
proposals.method: We will construct a heterogeneous graph from retrieved evidence, where nodes are entities and passages, and edges represent relations or semantic similarity. Using Graph Neural Networks (e.g., Graph Attention Networks) and probabilistic graphical models, we will propagate confidence scores from evidence nodes through the graph to candidate answer entities. This integrates with a transformer-based QA model, where the graph-derived uncertainty scores regularize the final answer distribution. The approach will be evaluated on benchmarks like HotpotQA and MuSiQue.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for uncertainty-aware, multi-hop reasoning that explicitly models relational structure. It will advance methods for explainable QA by pinpointing unreliable subgraphs in the reasoning chain and improve the performance and robustness of retrieval-augmented systems.
proposals.novelty: While graph methods exist for QA, their integration with explicit uncertainty quantification for dynamic, evidence-based reasoning graphs is novel. This bridges the gap between Lapata's prior work on uncertainty in QA and structured, hierarchical document analysis.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain Coreference Transfer for Coherent Entity Tracking in Long-Form Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Long-form text generation tasks, such as multi-document summarization and narrative creation, require consistent entity tracking across lengthy contexts. Current systems often fail to maintain coherent entity grids, leading to factual inconsistencies and degraded output quality. Transferring robust coreference resolution models to this structured tracking problem offers a principled solution to a key bottleneck in document-level NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art neural coreference resolution models, pre-trained on general-domain corpora, can be effectively adapted via transfer learning to generate accurate and coherent entity grids for long documents, thereby improving the factual consistency of downstream text generation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-2026 end-to-end neural coreference model (e.g., SpanBERT-based) as our base. The model will be fine-tuned on a dataset annotated with entity grids, mapping coreference chains to grid structures. We will employ a context-aware hierarchical merging strategy, inspired by Lapata's prior work, to aggregate entity mentions across document sections. Performance will be evaluated by integrating the generated grids into a retrieval-augmented QA system to measure improvements in answer consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel method for constructing entity grids via coreference transfer, directly applicable to summarization and story generation. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating entity consistency in long-form text and empirical evidence on the transferability of discourse-level models to structured representation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While coreference resolution is well-studied, its direct application and adaptation for structured entity grid generation is unexplored. The novelty lies in the cross-task transfer framework and the integration of hierarchical context modeling from coreference chains to a grid-based coherence metric.

proposals.title: CoherentFlow: Modeling Document-Level Topic Dynamics via Entity Transition Graphs
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for summarization and question answering excel at local coherence but struggle to capture the global thematic flow of long documents. This limits performance on tasks requiring understanding of narrative progression or argument structure, such as summarizing a scientific paper or answering complex, multi-faceted questions. A robust, scalable model of document-level topic coherence is a critical missing component for next-generation document understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the sequential transitions of salient entities and their relational contexts across a document provides a robust, interpretable signal for quantifying and generating globally coherent topic flow.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage neural-symbolic framework. First, we extract entities and their predicate-argument relations per sentence using a pre-2026 model like a fine-tuned T5 or a structured predictor. Second, we construct a temporal heterogeneous graph where nodes are entities and edges represent co-occurrence and typed semantic relations (e.g., 'acts-on', 'contrasts-with') across adjacent discourse units.
