We will train a Graph Neural Network (GNN) with temporal attention to learn transition patterns and predict a coherence score, integrating this as a regularization loss or re-ranking mechanism into Dr. Lapata's existing hierarchical summarization and QA architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, interpretable model for document-level coherence assessment based on entity dynamics, 2) an integration method to inject global coherence signals into generation and QA systems, and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating long-form topic flow, likely extending datasets like PubMed or WikiSum.
proposals.novelty: While entity chains are known, our approach novelly models typed relational transitions as a temporal graph, moving beyond simple coreference chains to capture the semantic *nature* of topic shifts, and directly integrates this into end-to-end neural generation frameworks for summarization and QA.

proposals.title: Inverse Coherence Modeling: Controllable Entity Grid Generation for Targeted Discourse Quality
proposals.motivation: Entity grid models, a classic pre-2026 discourse coherence metric, map text to a grid of entity transitions to predict readability. However, they are purely analytical. A generative inverse model would enable controllable text structuring, allowing systems to plan entity distributions for desired coherence levels before generation, directly benefiting tasks like long-form summarization and document planning where flow is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a conditional generative model can learn to produce plausible, diverse entity grid structures that, when realized as text, achieve a target coherence score, thereby inverting the traditional analysis pipeline.
proposals.method: We will frame this as a conditional sequence generation task. A transformer-based encoder will process a seed entity set and a target coherence score (e.g., from a pretrained entity grid scorer). A decoder, trained via policy gradient or Gumbel-Softmax relaxation, will autoregressively generate a sequence of entity role vectors (S/O/X/-) per sentence. Training will use a reconstruction loss against grids from a corpus (e.g., RST-DT) and a reward from the scorer, ensuring output grids match the target score.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce the first generative model for discourse structure planning via entity grids, providing a novel control mechanism for text generation systems. It will also offer a diagnostic tool for analyzing the sensitivity of coherence metrics by revealing which grid patterns they favor.
proposals.novelty: While entity grids are a well-established analysis tool, their inversion for controlled generation is unexplored. This bridges discriminative discourse analysis with controllable neural generation in a novel way, moving beyond using coherence merely as a post-hoc evaluation metric.

proposals.title: Simulated Grounding: Quantifying the Transfer of Embodied Reasoning from Rich Virtual Environments to Physical Robots
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in embodied AI is the high cost and slow data collection of physical robots. While simulation offers a scalable alternative, it remains unclear whether knowledge acquired in simulation can substitute for physical grounding in complex reasoning tasks. This work addresses a key question in sample-efficient robot learning and the nature of embodied intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent trained for question-answering and task planning within a rich, multi-modal simulated environment can transfer its learned reasoning to a physical robot with minimal fine-tuning, achieving performance comparable to an agent trained directly on physical data.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework, treating the simulator as a dynamic knowledge base. The agent will be trained on procedurally generated 'how-to' and 'why' questions within the simulation (e.g., Isaac Gym). We will employ context-aware hierarchical merging to integrate multi-sensory simulated data (vision, proprioception, physics) into a structured world model. Transfer performance will be rigorously evaluated by deploying the model on a physical robot arm, using uncertainty quantification metrics to assess the reliability of its transferred plans and answers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative benchmark for simulation-to-reality transfer of cognitive skills. It will contribute a new methodology for building and evaluating 'virtually grounded' world models, potentially reducing the need for extensive physical trials in robot learning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior sim-to-real work focused on low-level control, we test the transfer of high-level, language-mediated reasoning. Our approach uniquely integrates Lapata's expertise in QA, hierarchical context merging, and uncertainty quantification to rigorously evaluate the sufficiency of simulated grounding.

proposals.title: Evaluating Social Language Understanding in Embodied Contexts: A Benchmark for Interactive, Situated Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Current language understanding benchmarks primarily assess static text, neglecting the dynamic, situated nature of human communication. As AI systems move towards embodied agents (e.g., robots, virtual assistants), their ability to interpret and generate language grounded in physical context and social interaction becomes critical. Pre-2026, there is a lack of tasks that specifically evaluate how models process language that is contingent on a shared physical environment, social roles, and interactive dialogue history.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current state-of-the-art language models will show significant performance degradation on language understanding tasks when those tasks require reasoning about an embodied agent's physical situation and the social dynamics of an interaction, revealing a key limitation in their contextual grounding.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark comprising procedurally generated, multi-modal scenarios. Each scenario will include a 3D environment description, agent personas, and a dialogue history. The core task will be a **Context-Aware Hierarchical Question Answering** challenge, where models must answer questions about social implications, intentions, or subsequent actions, requiring **decomposed reasoning** over visual, spatial, and social context modules. We will adapt and extend Dr. Lapata's verified aspect-aware and uncertainty quantification methods to assess model confidence in its social and physical inferences, ensuring the benchmark tests robust, verifiable understanding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated benchmark for embodied social language understanding, moving beyond static text evaluation. It will yield a diagnostic tool to pinpoint specific failures in models' situational reasoning and offer a framework for integrating physical and social context into language model evaluation. The benchmark will catalyze research into more contextually grounded AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the challenges of embodied AI (physical grounding) with nuanced social language understanding into a single evaluation framework, moving beyond existing visual QA or social dialogue datasets which treat these aspects in isolation. The use of hierarchical, verified reasoning modules for assessment is also a novel methodological contribution to evaluation.

proposals.title: Structured Context Infusion: A Hybrid Symbolic-Neural Framework for Verifiable Long-Form Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Neural models, especially in retrieval-augmented QA, struggle with long, multi-faceted contexts, often producing unverifiable or inconsistent answers. Conversely, purely symbolic systems lack the flexibility to handle the ambiguity and scale of real-world data. A principled integration is needed to combine the reasoning strengths of symbolic methods with the representational power of neural networks for more reliable, context-aware generation.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the hierarchical and relational structure of a retrieved context using a symbolic graph, and using this graph to constrain and guide a neural language model's generation process, will significantly improve answer faithfulness, coherence, and verifiability in long-form QA.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture. First, a context parser extracts entities, claims, and their relations from retrieved passages, constructing a verified, aspect-aware symbolic graph (inspired by Lapata's verified modules). Second, a neural generator (e.g., a T5 or GPT variant) is conditioned on this graph via a novel hierarchical merging attention mechanism, where graph nodes and paths act as explicit, controllable prompts. The model will be trained with a combined loss for graph construction fidelity and answer quality, evaluated on datasets like ELI5 or QASPER.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, interpretable hybrid architecture for complex QA, a methodology for infusing structured symbolic knowledge into neural generation, and a demonstration of improved performance on metrics of factual consistency and answer completeness over purely neural or retrieval-based baselines.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior neuro-symbolic work that often uses knowledge bases, our method dynamically constructs task-specific symbolic graphs from retrieved text and uses a context-aware hierarchical merging mechanism to directly control neural generation, creating a tighter, more verifiable integration loop.

proposals.title: Modeling Conversational Structure with Sociological Discourse Analysis for Improved Long-Form Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Current retrieval-augmented QA systems often treat dialogue as a sequence of isolated utterances, missing the higher-order social and rhetorical structures that govern real human conversation. This limits their ability to answer complex, multi-turn questions that require understanding argumentative flow, power dynamics, or consensus-building. Grounding interaction modeling in established sociological theory offers a principled way to capture these latent structures, moving beyond surface-level patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the sociological discourse structure of multi-party conversations—such as turn-taking rituals, face-work, and argumentation schemes—will improve the performance and robustness of retrieval-augmented models on long-form, context-dependent question answering tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical encoder that integrates sociological discourse analysis. First, we adapt frameworks like Conversation Analysis (e.g., adjacency pairs, repair sequences) and Critical Discourse Analysis to annotate existing long dialogue corpora (e.g., AMI, Debatepedia). We then design a two-tier transformer architecture: a lower-level encoder processes utterances, and a higher-level 'discourse graph' encoder, informed by the annotations, models sociological relations (e.g., challenge, support, clarification). This graph structure will condition the retrieval and reasoning modules of a model like FiD or RAG, with uncertainty quantification applied to the predicted discourse links.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable framework for encoding sociological discourse in dialogue models and release a new annotated benchmark for social dialogue understanding. It will demonstrate measurable gains in QA performance on tasks requiring tracking of claims, agreements, and social dynamics over long contexts.
proposals.novelty: While recent work merges information hierarchically, it does not ground the merging process in formal sociological theory of interaction. This proposal uniquely uses discourse analysis to define the latent variables and relational structure for merging conversational context, moving beyond purely data-driven adjacency.

proposals.title: Syntax-Guided Sketch Generation for Coherent and Faithful Abstractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Abstractive summarization models often generate incoherent or factually inconsistent text, particularly in multi-document or long-document settings. While sketch-based methods, which first generate a coarse outline, improve structure, they are typically based on semantic heuristics, ignoring the explicit syntactic scaffolding of source text. Incorporating syntax can provide a stronger inductive bias for generating well-formed and content-grounded sketches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using syntactic parses (e.g., dependency trees or constituency parses) to inform the generation of an initial coarse sketch will lead to summaries with improved grammatical coherence, factual consistency, and structural fidelity to the source documents.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage encoder-decoder architecture. First, we encode source documents using a transformer encoder and extract syntactic parses using a pre-2026 parser (e.g., Stanford CoreNLP or spaCy). A graph neural network will encode the dependency structure, and its output will be fused with the semantic encoder states via attention. This joint representation will condition a decoder to generate a 'syntax-aware sketch'—a sequence of simplified syntactic templates or key content phrases. This sketch will then be expanded by a second decoder into the final summary, with the sketch acting as a hard constraint during generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, syntax-informed framework for sketch-based summarization, demonstrating measurable gains in coherence and faithfulness. It will also provide insights into how explicit linguistic structure can be effectively integrated into neural generation pipelines for improved controllability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in summarization uses syntax for salience detection or post-hoc correction, our approach is novel in directly leveraging syntactic parses to generate the initial planning sketch, creating a stronger structural bridge between source and target text.

proposals.title: Coarse-to-Fine Semantic Parsing for Complex Formal Representations: A Verified, Hierarchical Approach
proposals.motivation: Coarse-to-fine parsing has shown promise for SQL and lambda calculus, but its efficacy for more complex, structured meaning representations (e.g., Discourse Representation Structures (DRS), Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR), or functional program sketches) remains unexplored. These representations are crucial for advanced reasoning and generation tasks, yet they pose significant challenges due to their deep hierarchies and compositional constraints. Extending this paradigm can address the scalability and accuracy bottlenecks in semantic parsing for high-stakes applications like complex question answering and formal verification.
proposals.hypothesis: A context-aware, hierarchical coarse-to-fine parsing framework, augmented with uncertainty quantification and verified module selection, will significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of generating complex formal semantic representations compared to flat or monolithic generation methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the hierarchical merging and decomposition principles from Lapata's recent work. The method involves: 1) A coarse generator (e.g., a seq2seq model) that produces an underspecified sketch of the target representation (e.g., a DRS with placeholder predicates). 2) A set of verified, aspect-aware refinement modules (inspired by decomposed summarization) that fill in the sketch, each specialized for a specific semantic category (e.g., temporal relations, negation). 3) A context-aware merging mechanism to compose the refined components, with uncertainty quantification (as in retrieval-augmented QA) used to route ambiguous cases for human verification or iterative refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evaluation of coarse-to-fine techniques on a broad set of complex semantic formalisms, establishing new benchmarks. It will deliver a flexible, modular parsing framework that improves interpretability and control. The integration of verification and uncertainty estimation will also enhance the reliability of semantic parsers for downstream reasoning tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of verified modular decomposition and uncertainty-aware hierarchical merging—techniques proven in summarization and QA—to the novel domain of generating rich, non-procedural semantic representations, moving beyond the well-trodden paths of SQL and lambda calculus.

proposals.title: Iterative Refinement for Factual Consistency in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models often produce outputs with factual inconsistencies or incomplete detail integration from retrieved contexts, undermining reliability. Current methods typically generate answers in a single pass, lacking a mechanism for post-hoc verification and correction. This is a critical bottleneck for high-stakes applications like question answering and summarization, where accuracy is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying iterative, edit-based refinement techniques from neural machine translation to the detail-filling stage of RAG will systematically reduce factual errors and improve the completeness of generated text compared to single-pass generation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Levenshtein Transformer or an iterative non-autoregressive decoder, pre-trained for machine translation, to the text generation domain. The model will take an initial draft answer (from a standard RAG system) and the retrieved evidence as input, performing multiple refinement steps. Each step will involve a learned editor that proposes local insertions, deletions, or substitutions to better align the draft with the source evidence, guided by a fact-verification reward signal. This will be integrated into the hierarchical merging framework from Lapata's prior work for multi-document contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, general-purpose refinement module for RAG that explicitly targets factual consistency. It will provide a new evaluation benchmark for iterative improvement in knowledge-intensive NLP tasks and offer insights into the transferability of MT refinement strategies to conditional generation.
proposals.novelty: While iterative decoding is established in MT, its application to correct factual grounding errors in RAG is unexplored. The key innovation is framing evidence-based detail-filling as a sequence-to-sequence editing problem, moving beyond one-shot generation or simple post-hoc reranking.

proposals.title: Coarse-to-Fine Semantic Parsing for Very Long Documents via Hierarchical Graph Decomposition
proposals.motivation: Semantic parsing for long, multi-sentence documents (e.g., scientific papers, legal contracts) remains a major challenge. Existing neural parsers, designed for sentences or short paragraphs, struggle with the scale and complex discourse structure of such inputs, leading to information loss and poor performance. This limits the ability to construct accurate, queryable knowledge bases from real-world textual sources.
proposals.hypothesis: A coarse-to-fine parsing strategy, which first constructs a high-level document graph of core entities and relations and then recursively refines sub-graphs for detailed semantic content, will significantly outperform flat, sequence-to-graph models on very long documents.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture. First, a coarse encoder (e.g., a Longformer) processes the full document to produce a latent discourse tree and identify core entity clusters. A context-aware hierarchical merging module, inspired by Lapata's summarization work, condenses this representation. Then, a fine-grained decoder, guided by verified aspect-aware modules from opinion summarization, performs targeted, local parsing on selected text spans to populate detailed, executable logical forms (e.g., AMR, DRS). The process is iterative, with uncertainty quantification from Lapata's QA research used to decide which regions require further refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable framework for document-level semantic parsing, pushing the boundary of input length for structured meaning representation. It will provide a new benchmark dataset for long-document parsing and insights into integrating discourse analysis with semantic parsing. The method's modularity offers a blueprint for handling other complex, long-form NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: While coarse-to-fine is known in parsing, its application to *document-level* semantic parsing via a learned, hierarchical graph decomposition is novel. The key innovation is the tight integration of discourse-level planning (coarse) with verified, aspect-aware semantic module execution (fine), a synthesis not present in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Sketch-and-Fill: Data-Efficient Semantic Parsing for Low-Resource Languages via Decomposed Uncertainty Quantification
proposals.motivation: Semantic parsing is crucial for enabling complex reasoning and question answering in non-English languages, but creating high-quality, large-scale datasets for each language is prohibitively expensive. Current cross-lingual transfer methods often fail when target language data is extremely scarce, leading to poor performance and high uncertainty in model outputs. This work addresses the critical need for sample-efficient methods that can bootstrap accurate semantic parsers from minimal annotations.
proposals.hypothesis: A sketch-based decomposition of the parsing task, where a high-level logical form 'sketch' is generated first and then 'filled' with language-specific arguments, will significantly reduce data requirements for low-resource languages by isolating and quantifying transferable structural knowledge from language-specific lexical knowledge.
proposals.method: We adapt a context-aware hierarchical merging framework to semantic parsing. First, a multilingual encoder generates an abstract, language-agnostic sketch of the logical form (e.g., predicate-argument structure). This sketch is verified by an aspect-aware module trained on high-resource languages. Second, a separate, lightweight filler module, trained on the limited target-language data, instantiates the sketch with concrete lexical items. We will integrate uncertainty quantification (e.g., Bayesian deep learning or ensemble methods) at both stages to identify where the model lacks confidence, enabling targeted data collection or prompting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-efficient pipeline for semantic parsing in low-resource settings, directly extending the PI's expertise in decomposition and uncertainty. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for evaluating cross-lingual parsing under extreme data scarcity and offer insights into which semantic structures transfer most effectively across languages.
proposals.novelty: While sketch-based methods exist for English, their application to cross-lingual parsing with integrated uncertainty quantification is novel. The key innovation is treating the sketch as a transferable, verifiable intermediate representation whose reliability can be measured, thereby reducing the annotation burden for the full parse.

ideas.title: Automatic metrics sufficiently evaluate abstractive summaries
ideas.description: Develop new evaluation frameworks focusing on factual consistency and grounding.
ideas.sourceTitle: Don’t Give Me the Details, Just the Summary! Topic-Aware Convolutional Neural Networks for Extreme Summarization

ideas.title: Dual task: summary expansion
ideas.description: Use the inverse model to generate a detailed document from a short summary.
ideas.sourceTitle: Neural Summarization by Extracting Sentences and Words

ideas.title: Topic labels are necessary for good summarization
ideas.description: Build a model that performs extreme summarization without any explicit topic conditioning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Don’t Give Me the Details, Just the Summary! Topic-Aware Convolutional Neural Networks for Extreme Summarization

ideas.title: Adapt Clinical Trial Adaptive Randomization for Structured RL Exploration in Summarization
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from clinical trial design—specifically, response-adaptive randomization—to the problem of efficient exploration in RL for summarization. In clinical trials, adaptive randomization dynamically allocates more patients to the treatment arm showing better preliminary outcomes to reduce variance in efficacy estimates and minimize patient exposure to inferior treatments. Adapted to RL for summarization, the agent's exploration policy is structured to dynamically bias its action selection (e.g., towards certain summary styles or operations) based on interim reward signals, while maintaining a controlled level of exploration across all actions. This creates a guided, variance-reduced exploration strategy that allows the RL model to concentrate its sampling on more promising summarization behaviors earlier in training, leading to more stable and sample-efficient policy learning compared to standard contextual bandit or undirected RL exploration approaches.

ideas.title: Investigate induced token relations
ideas.description: Analyze and formalize the relations weakly induced among tokens by the adaptive memory and attention.
ideas.sourceTitle: Long Short-Term Memory-Networks for Machine Reading

ideas.title: Scale down to few-shot or zero-shot summarization
ideas.description: Adapt the RL framework for domains with very limited or no training data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Ranking Sentences for Extractive Summarization with Reinforcement Learning

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Theory for Submodular Diversity Selection
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using submodular optimization for diverse selection. By analogy, this problem closely resembles the challenge in Financial Portfolio Optimization of selecting a diverse set of non-redundant assets to manage risk, solved by Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT). MPT quantifies diversity through covariance matrices and optimizes for it directly. Adapting this solution, we propose designing a submodular function that explicitly incorporates a similarity matrix or kernel between candidate items. The objective function would be a weighted sum of a traditional monotone submodular coverage term and a dispersion term that penalizes the selection of highly similar items, directly promoting diversity and reducing redundancy in the selected subset, much like MPT balances return and correlation risk.

ideas.title: Incorporate syntax into sketch generation
ideas.description: Use syntactic parses to inform the generation of the initial coarse sketch.
ideas.sourceTitle: Coarse-to-Fine Decoding for Neural Semantic Parsing

ideas.title: Scale up to extremely long document summarization
ideas.description: Modify architecture and training for books, legal documents, or scientific articles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Ranking Sentences for Extractive Summarization with Reinforcement Learning

ideas.title: Diagnostic Framework for Isolating and Remedying RL Ranking Failure Modes Across Domains
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple cross-domain testing to build a diagnostic framework that identifies why an RL ranking approach fails on new domains and languages. It creates a suite of testbeds that systematically vary key axes of generalization, such as query-document relationship, user behavior patterns, and linguistic features. The framework then runs controlled experiments to isolate failure modes to specific system components, like the reward model's bias or the policy's lack of adaptability. The final output is a set of targeted mitigation strategies, enabling the construction of more robust and fundamentally generalizable RL ranking systems.

ideas.title: A Human-Calibrated, Claim-Driven Framework for Validating Automatic Summary Evaluation Metrics
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid evaluation framework that directly anchors automatic metrics to human judgment on factual consistency and grounding. The system first decomposes summaries into atomic, verifiable claims and uses uncertainty estimation to select the most critical and challenging claims for minimal human annotation. These human judgments are then used not as a gold standard for scoring, but as calibration data to validate, weight, and explain the outputs of a suite of existing and novel automatic metrics. The result is a transparent and robust evaluation system that quantifies the reliability of automatic scores and specifies the contexts in which they can be trusted, moving beyond developing metrics in isolation to building validated measurement tools.

ideas.title: Use reinforcement learning for summary optimization
ideas.description: Incorporate RL to directly optimize for non-differentiable metrics like ROUGE or readability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Don’t Give Me the Details, Just the Summary! Topic-Aware Convolutional Neural Networks for Extreme Summarization

ideas.title: Explicitly Grounded Generation via Differentiable and Verifiable Span Selection
ideas.description: This approach redesigns abstractive generation to begin with an explicit, trainable span selection module that operates over the source text. The module produces a sparse, differentiable attention mask identifying relevant source spans, which is then used to condition the generator. The system is trained with dual objectives: one for accurate span selection (verifiable against ground-truth extracts) and one for fluent generation conditioned on those spans, ensuring the final output is transparently grounded in identifiable source material.

ideas.title: Cross-Domain and Cross-Lingual Stress Testing via Ecological Survey Methods
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to rigorously evaluate the generalizability of the RL ranking approach by drawing an analogy from ecology. In biodiversity monitoring, ecologists conduct systematic surveys across diverse biomes (e.g., rainforests, deserts) using standardized protocols to assess species resilience. Adapted to this research context, we treat different data domains (legal, social media, scientific) and languages as analogous to distinct ecological biomes. The solution is to create a standardized evaluation 'transect'—a fixed battery of robustness and performance metrics—and apply the RL model uniformly across this deliberately constructed landscape of diverse, non-news datasets. This cross-domain and cross-lingual stress testing will systematically map the model's 'adaptive niche,' pinpointing which domain-language combinations act as critical stressors and thereby revealing the fundamental boundaries and transferable core of its ranking capabilities.

ideas.title: Unsupervised Topic Discovery via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology to improve unsupervised topic representation learning. In ecology, latent structures like ecological niches are inferred from species co-occurrence data across environmental gradients without pre-defined habitat categories, using techniques such as ordination. The adapted research idea applies this analogical framework to documents: treating documents as sites, words as species, and semantic dimensions as environmental gradients. By analyzing term co-occurrence patterns with gradient-based methods (e.g., adapting Non-metric Multidimensional Scaling for text), the goal is to discover continuous, latent 'topic niches' that represent the fundamental semantic dimensions shaping document content, moving beyond discrete, pre-defined topic tags.

ideas.title: Create a lightweight, real-time version via computational stencil optimization
ideas.description: This idea proposes creating a lightweight, real-time model version by drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), where 'stencil optimization' is used to make complex simulations tractable in near-real-time. The analogous solution involves deeply optimizing the core computational kernels (stencils) for specific hardware through loop transformations and memory access patterns. Adapted to machine learning, the research would focus on identifying and hand-optimizing the fundamental, repeated operations (e.g., specific tensor contractions, non-linearities) of a model for a target edge device's SoC. By building a model from these ultra-efficient, hardware-tailored primitives—rather than relying on generic graph compilers—the approach aims to achieve significantly lower latency and higher throughput for real-time inference on mobile and edge devices, directly addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension of performance versus resource constraints.

ideas.title: Scale to multimodal reading
ideas.description: Extend the incremental reading simulator to jointly process text with visual or audio signals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Long Short-Term Memory-Networks for Machine Reading

ideas.title: Create a fast, approximate reading model
ideas.description: Develop a simplified, high-speed variant for tasks where perfect accuracy is not required.
ideas.sourceTitle: Long Short-Term Memory-Networks for Machine Reading

ideas.title: Multi-Perspective Summary Scaffolds for Mitigating Filter Bubbles
ideas.description: This system generates not a single personalized summary, but a structured scaffold of multiple summary perspectives derived from the same source material. It automatically identifies key claims and constructs distinct narrative summaries conditioned on different viewpoints, stakeholder interests, or epistemic lenses. The output presents these summaries in a comparative interface that visually maps agreements, contradictions, and informational gaps between them. This design ensures users are exposed to the inherent complexity and potential biases within the information, promoting critical evaluation over streamlined consumption.

ideas.title: Adversarial Metric Robust Training for Text Generation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the adversarial robustness framework from computer vision to address metric bias in NLP evaluation. In vision, models are made robust to input perturbations through adversarial training that doesn't assume clean inputs. Analogously, for text generation, we develop evaluation-aware training that doesn't assume ROUGE (or similar metrics) are perfect gold proxies. We implement an adversarial training setup where a generator model produces text, and a critic model is trained to distinguish between human-preferred text and text optimized purely for ROUGE scores. The generator is then trained with a dual objective: achieving high ROUGE scores while also fooling the critic into judging its outputs as human-like. This forces the model to learn representations that capture qualities humans value but that ROUGE misses, making the training robust to the metric's known biases toward surface-level lexical overlap.

ideas.title: Coherent Neural Topic Flow for Document Ranking
ideas.description: This research develops neural ranking models that jointly infer a document's latent, evolving topic structure and model the inter-sentence dependencies that create its thematic coherence. Instead of treating sentences as independent or using static global topics, the model learns to represent how sentences sequentially develop and transition between subtopics, forming a coherent 'topic flow'. This allows the ranking function to directly reward documents that not only contain relevant concepts but also present them in a logically structured and thematically consistent narrative, fundamentally moving beyond bag-of-words or independent sentence assumptions.

ideas.title: Seismic Tomography-Inspired Context Selection for Document Expansion
ideas.description: This research adapts seismic tomography's solution to inverse problems for document expansion. In seismology, limited surface measurements are inverted to reconstruct detailed 3D subsurface structures through iterative forward modeling, comparison with observed data, and regularization. Similarly, we propose an iterative expansion system where: (1) The ranking model initially selects context from a corpus to generate a document section; (2) This generated section is compared against the original summary's semantic 'waveform' (embedding patterns); (3) Discrepancies guide the next iteration's context selection through regularization toward narrative coherence and factual consistency; (4) The process repeats until the expanded document optimally 'explains' the summary while maintaining internal consistency. This creates a dynamic scaling dual where context selection and generation continuously inform each other, moving beyond one-shot expansion toward structurally coherent document reconstruction.

ideas.title: Optimizing Text Generation with Multi-Objective Reward Shaping from Computational Neuroscience
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from computational neuroscience, where models of the brain's reward system use multi-objective reward shaping to combine intrinsic and extrinsic signals. The analogous problem is designing an agent's reward function to satisfy multiple biological constraints, solved by creating a composite reward. Adapted to NLP, the idea is to design and test a reinforcement learning reward for text generation that is a weighted blend of several automated metrics (capturing semantics, syntax, factuality) alongside intermittent human preference feedback. This shaped reward function would guide a language model to optimize for a richer set of qualities than surface-level n-gram overlap (ROUGE), mimicking the brain's integrative reward processing.

ideas.title: Scale up to document-level topic coherence

ideas.title: Grounding Unsupervised Topic Models via Conceptual Anchor Regularization
ideas.description: This research develops a method to learn document topics without pre-defined tags while preventing the semantic drift common in purely statistical models. It achieves this by weakly constraining the unsupervised learning process with automatically gathered conceptual anchors from external knowledge sources. The system regularizes the topic representations to remain interpretably aligned with these anchors, ensuring meaningful coherence. This results in topics that are both data-driven and reliably grounded for practical use.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Strategy for Summary Optimization via Policy Gradients
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology and population genetics, where adaptation occurs via selection on a non-differentiable fitness landscape. The analogous computational solution is Evolutionary Strategies (ES), a population-based black-box optimization method. We adapt this by proposing a Natural Evolution Strategy for summary optimization: a population of parameter vectors, created by perturbing the weights of a summarization policy network, is sampled; each parameter set generates summaries that are scored by non-differentiable metrics (ROUGE, readability); the central policy is then updated by moving its parameters in the direction of the weighted sum of the perturbations, with weights proportional to the obtained scores. This provides a robust, gradient-free alternative to standard RL policy gradients for directly optimizing discrete text generation against complex evaluation metrics.

ideas.title: Adapting Structural Equation Modeling from Psychology to Build a Causal Framework for Summary Evaluation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that automatic metrics sufficiently evaluate abstractive summaries by drawing an analogy from psychometrics. In psychology, complex latent constructs like intelligence are not measured directly but are inferred from multiple, imperfect observable tests using frameworks like Structural Equation Modeling (SEM). Analogously, we propose to treat summary qualities such as 'factual consistency' and 'grounding' as latent constructs. The adapted research idea is to develop an evaluation framework that models these latent qualities and their causal relationships with a battery of existing automatic metrics (e.g., BERTScore, QAE, entailment scores) and human judgments. By statistically assessing the model fit, this framework can test whether the constellation of current automatic metrics sufficiently captures the underlying constructs of interest, thereby providing a principled, evidence-based challenge to the prevailing assumption rather than a purely qualitative one.

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning for Iterative Sketch Refinement in Multi-Document Summarization
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a correct, minimal sketch is the optimal starting point for multi-document summarization. Instead, we propose a reinforcement learning ranking framework where the agent learns to generate and iteratively refine content sketches of varying complexity and potential initial inaccuracy. The reward function is designed to evaluate the long-term utility of a sketch based on its final refined summary's quality, balancing the cost of sketch generation against gains in information integration and hallucination reduction across multiple source documents. This treats sketch generation not as a simpler pre-processing step, but as a core, learnable strategic decision within the summarization pipeline.

ideas.title: Adaptive Information Filtering via Conceptual Compressive Sensing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single summary can fit all by adapting the solution from compressive sensing in signal processing. In that field, a complete signal is recovered from very few samples by leveraging knowledge of the domain in which the signal is sparse. Analogously, we propose treating a full document as a dense 'information signal.' A user's interest or query defines a 'conceptual sparsity basis'—a specific domain (e.g., technical details, financial implications, historical context) in which the relevant information for that user is concentrated. The research would develop methods to take minimal, adaptive 'measurements' of the document (e.g., selecting key embeddings or propositions) conditioned on the inferred user basis. A reconstruction algorithm, perhaps based on sparse coding or attention mechanisms, would then synthesize these measurements into a personalized extreme summary. Thus, the system doesn't generate a one-size-fits-all summary but learns to efficiently sense and reconstruct the information uniquely valuable to each user.

ideas.title: Signal Denoising for Efficient Information Extraction
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from signal processing to scale a dual-task system for question answering. In telecommunications, a filter is used as a preprocessing step to denoise a signal, allowing the receiver to operate more efficiently on the clean data. Similarly, we propose using the sentence importance ranking model as a semantic filter for QA systems. This filter preprocesses a long document or context, stripping away peripheral or irrelevant sentences and passing only a ranked subset of the most important ones to the core QA model. This adaptation transforms the ranking task into a critical preprocessing stage that denoises the textual input, enabling the QA system to scale to longer documents, focus its reasoning capacity, and potentially achieve higher accuracy with lower computational expense.

ideas.title: Iterative, Question-Guided Context Refinement for QA
ideas.description: This system integrates sentence relevance scoring directly into the QA pipeline through a tight, iterative loop. Instead of a one-off preprocessing step, a lightweight relevance model first retrieves a broad set of potentially relevant sentences. The core QA model then processes this set, and its internal attention or confidence signals are used to guide a second pass, where the relevance model refetches or up-weights sentences from the full context that support or contrast with the initial answer hypothesis. This closed-loop process continues until convergence, dynamically focusing on question-critical information without imposing a hard, early filter.

ideas.title: Dynamic Summary Length via Adaptive Signal Sampling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the fixed-length summary assumption by integrating content sufficiency modeling to dynamically determine summary length. The analogy is drawn from Digital Signal Processing and Compression, specifically adaptive sampling techniques like variable bitrate encoding, where the sampling rate is adjusted based on the signal's complexity. The adapted solution treats the source document as an information signal. A model analyzes the text's local and global information density, identifying complex, novel, or critical segments versus redundant or simple ones. The summarization process then performs adaptive 'sampling,' allocating more summary length (tokens) to dense regions and less to sparse ones. A separate content sufficiency model, trained to evaluate the fidelity of the compressed signal (summary) against the original's core meaning, determines the stopping point. The summary length thus emerges dynamically, optimized for the specific content rather than constrained by a pre-set limit.

ideas.title: Neural Attention as a Soft, Differentiable Selection Operator
ideas.description: This idea inverts the extractive-only assumption by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience models of visual attention. In those models, attention acts as a soft, differentiable selection operator—a 'spotlight' that weights sensory inputs—rather than a hard switch. The analogous solution is a learned attention map that multiplicatively gates feature maps. Adapted to NLP, the research idea proposes to treat extraction as a latent, soft selection step within an abstractive generation process. Specifically, a model would be designed to learn a continuous attention distribution over source text (the 'extractive' component), which directly and differentially modulates the context for a neural generator. This integrates extraction and abstraction into a single, end-to-end differentiable process, challenging the assumption that they must be separate, discrete stages.

ideas.title: Edge-Adaptive Neural Network with Real-Time Online Calibration
ideas.description: This research designs a lightweight model architecture specifically for edge deployment that couples a fast inference engine with a minimal-footprint online adaptation system. The system continuously evaluates performance metrics and detects data distribution shifts in real-time. Upon detecting drift, it activates an efficient fine-tuning routine using a reserved compute budget and a sliding window of recent inputs, updating only a small subset of parameters to preserve accuracy. This ensures the model remains robust and reliable in non-stationary environments without compromising its low-latency core operation.

ideas.title: Condition Summarizer on a Latent Topic Map via Topographic VAE
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using VAEs to learn a continuous latent topic space for conditioning a summarizer. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, where topographic VAEs are used to create structured, navigable latent maps of brain activity (e.g., cognitive maps from fMRI data), we adapt this solution. We propose employing a topographic VAE with a structured prior (e.g., on a manifold like a hypersphere) to learn a latent topic space where geometry reflects topic semantics. This creates a smooth, interpretable 'topic map.' The summarizer is then conditioned on coordinates within this map, allowing for precise control over the thematic focus of generated summaries by selecting or interpolating points along the learned manifold.

ideas.title: Lightweight Latent-Selective Generation for Real-Time Edge Summarization
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel architecture for real-time text summarization on mobile and edge devices. It fuses the need for low-latency inference with a model that inverts the traditional extract-then-abstract pipeline. Instead, the model performs a single, highly optimized forward pass where a lightweight attention mechanism acts as a latent, soft selector over the input, directly guiding a constrained abstractive decoder. This eliminates the computational overhead of separate extraction modules, enabling fluent, context-aware summarization under strict latency and resource constraints previously dominated by extractive-only models.

ideas.title: Adversarially Regularized Reward Modeling for Robust Text Generation
ideas.description: This research designs a reinforcement learning reward system that combines a dense reward model, trained on multi-dimensional human preferences, with active adversarial regularization during policy optimization. The adversarial component uses a battery of probes to generate examples that exploit potential weaknesses in the reward model (e.g., favoring plausible but unfaithful text). The policy is then trained to maximize the primary reward while simultaneously minimizing its score on these adversarial examples, as judged by the reward model itself. This forces the policy to learn representations that align with the underlying human preferences rather than superficial correlates, leading to more robust and higher-quality generation.
